|
Peter,
I always knew running a campaign that actually challenges Trump's fascist agenda would make me a target for MAGA.
But lately? My team is being flooded with hateful and even violent comments from MAGA extremists--people calling me "un-American," "a terrorist," and even telling me to "go back to my country."
---
| | [](https://e96gjzhbb.cc.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001b7domjW-pi3HAaLGgyks20RjsUo82yAa5CZdyPDdoCXgLaZO8Cqgix4txB17It_zoyXH74HurlNUz11_HgNbqaOiqprLfuwiiwxmTYCEFdDisvNdV_i4ONrG_fuEBKXFozYYe_Nkp08MrwWeuAjpTHBLElOhOkrpMMMzn8LGvaPt_jPOVGID72ZIk1vBqpq329hobQLMPnEEXqvINpy35tE4JDQheTy-&c=GzmV2mqpGQGvad_61G4fdIqqmn7RaAW94DmQnc0UrhVEmgeCgZkRNg==&ch=OuwB7ePZwDZLnIXqYpoxwZhdMdIek-D-zakMeiMeVlbYYFx9KLS5eQ==)

| I'm an American citizen. I was born here. And I love this country enough to fight like hell for it.
But apparently, standing up to Trump's abuses of power, protecting our community from ICE, and refusing to be a billionaire's puppet makes me some kind of threat. You know what? They're actually right about that part.
Because our movement is a threat--to Trump's fascist agenda, to his enablers, and to every politician (Democrat or Republican) who rolls over as this administration tramples over the Constitution.
We're building a movement that won't "meet fascism halfway."
We're done with performative resistance and empty statements.
We're done with Democrats who shrink from the moment when history demands they rise.
We're fighting for real opposition to fascism--for communities living in fear, for immigrants being hunted, and for everyone who's tired of watching "moderates" roll over while our rights disappear.
So when MAGA trolls flood my mentions, I see proof we're doing something right.
[If you want leaders who don't flinch in the face of hate -- who'll rise up to meet the threat Trump poses head-on -- I need you in this fight with me.](https://e96gjzhbb.cc.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001b7domjW-pi3HAaLGgyks20RjsUo82yAa5CZdyPDdoCXgLaZO8Cqgix4txB17It_z7uRKIai2oBr6GtkdreiDUwHjzmfGKO6KEJQmKm3-CMLguF3pLxJj2F5DdkJc9GcP&c=GzmV2mqpGQGvad_61G4fdIqqmn7RaAW94DmQnc0UrhVEmgeCgZkRNg==&ch=OuwB7ePZwDZLnIXqYpoxwZhdMdIek-D-zakMeiMeVlbYYFx9KLS5eQ==)

name: Craig Riedel for State Senate
subject: Riedel Campaign December Update
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Friends of Craig Riedel
body:
| | | | |
---
Riedel Campaign Update | December 19, 2025
---
Riedel Campaign Receives Endorsements
---
|
---
|
This month, the Riedel campaign received endorsements from both Ohio Value Voters and the Associated Builders and Contractors of Ohio. Both of these organizations look for candidates to support who will defend conservative values in the state of Ohio. Whether it's issues like defending the right to life, keeping men out of women's sports, lowering your taxes and keeping Ohio business friendly, Craig's proven voting record shows he is a battle-tested conservative champion.
Craig is grateful and encouraged by both of these endorsements and looks forward to working hard everyday to make Ohio strong, free and prosperous as your next State Senator.
---
|
---
| Craig and Danette Attend Logan County Fall Dinner

Craig and Danette were thrilled to attend the Logan County Fall Dinner last month. It wrapped up the fall season for banquets and dinners across the 1st Senate District. State Treasurer and candidate for Secretary of State Robert Sprague was the event's key-note speaker. Sprague shared his vision for the Secretary of State's office and shared what it means to be an Ohio conservative.
---
| | |
---
|
---
| | |
---
| |
---
| Craig Attends Meet and Greets in Paulding and Defiance
Craig was happy to participate in candidate meet and greets in Paulding and Defiance Counties. Craig shared his beliefs and what he hopes to accomplish as Northwest Ohio's next State Senator.
---
|
---
| |
---
| |
---
| | Riedel Campaign Ground Game Continues

As the weather continues to get colder, the Riedel campaign is still working tremendously hard knocking on doors all over the district. Voters are excited to hear about Craig's campaign and what he hopes to accomplish as your next state senator.
---
| | |
---
| |
---
|
# Craig and Danette Walk in Ottawa Santa Parade

Craig and Danette had a great time at the Ottawa Santa Christmas Parade. Even though it was cold, the people of Ottawa showed up to celebrate the Christmas season with friends and family. Along the parade route, Craig was able to meet voters and hand out candy to kids.
---
| |
---

---
| |
---

---
| |
---

---
| |
---
| | Craig and Danette Attend Williams County Township Dinner

Craig and Danette enjoyed their time at the Williams County Township Dinner last month. Williams County officials provided updates on what they have been working on this year and what they hope to accomplish in the coming months. Craig enjoyed the time of fellowship with friends from all over Williams County.
---
| |
---
| |
---

Trump's plot for a third term just won't die.
Last month, Defend Our Courts helped lead the defense of Pennsylvania's Supreme Court. We stopped MAGA from seizing control of the court that oversees how elections are run in the nation's biggest swing state. It was a blowout victory and a crucial win for democracy.
But what happened at the White House yesterday made it clear that the battle against a Trump third term is far from over. Trump has a fallback plan, and it runs straight through the courts in Arizona, Georgia, and Michigan.
In 2026, the Supreme Courts of these three critical states are on the ballot. If MAGA can buy these seats, they won't need to win an election in 2028, they'll just have their hand-picked judges throw out results they don't like.
The threat didn't go away after PA. It just tripled.
Defend Our Courts is once again leading the vanguard, rallying our over 100,000 grassroots supporters from every corner of this country to defend these three crucial courts.
But we cannot fight three fires at once without your immediate help. The stakes are higher than they have ever been.

# [Compromise is not a starting position. Compromise is an outcome. ](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3159258&post_id=181828141&utm_source=post-email-title&utm_campaign=email-post-title&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgxODI4MTQxLCJpYXQiOjE3NjYxNDMwMjcsImV4cCI6MTc2ODczNTAyNywiaXNzIjoicHViLTMxNTkyNTgiLCJzdWIiOiJwb3N0LXJlYWN0aW9uIn0.3bE7d4doUJFz0_tvPoCRUUhTPfZuiBpUAg0oelMfTL4)
### Representation starts with conviction.
| | [The Angry Democrat](https://substack.com/@theangrydem)
---
| Dec 19
---
| [](https://substack.com/@theangrydem)
---

---
| | | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=3159258&post_id=181828141&utm_source=substack&isFreemail=true&submitLike=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgxODI4MTQxLCJyZWFjdGlvbiI6IuKdpCIsImlhdCI6MTc2NjE0MzAyNywiZXhwIjoxNzY4NzM1MDI3LCJpc3MiOiJwdWItMzE1OTI1OCIsInN1YiI6InJlYWN0aW9uIn0.BrdPK-6LGWFkHQJzSlOmu-7HilQg5U5YnJF20WX-sg4&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=email-reaction&r=1hvr60)

| | [](https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9vcGVuLnN1YnN0YWNrLmNvbS9wdWIvbWF0dGRpZW1lci9wL2NvbXByb21pc2UtaXMtbm90LWEtc3RhcnRpbmctcG9zaXRpb24_.6KG4132RDNkLeUIjrqaKinCoK8TqHMi9Vg4J6SdGbsc?&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email)

| | [READ IN APP](https://open.substack.com/pub/mattdiemer/p/compromise-is-not-a-starting-position?utm_source=email&redirect=app-store&utm_campaign=email-read-in-app)
---

**The Myth of Compromise**
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/47feb0f4-67e6-4f86-9c1c-d617caac73a4?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
One of the most popular lines candidates use when running for office is "I want to work across the aisle" or "I want to compromise." "I want to bring both sides together." It sounds reasonable. It sounds mature. And in practice, it is often a trap for weakness and failure.
I say that as someone who loves debate. I studied politics. I seriously considered law school. I enjoy arguments for their own sake. I consume right-wing media, left-wing media, centrist commentary, and everything in between because I believe intellectual curiosity makes you sharper, more empathetic, and better informed. Exploring ideas, morals, ethics, and ideology is healthy. It makes you smarter.
But there is a massive difference between intellectual exploration and governing.
A representative is not elected to endlessly explore ideas. A representative is elected to pick a position, fight for it, and deliver results for their constituents.
When a candidate runs on "seeing both sides" as their core message, what I hear is this: I do not believe strongly in anything, and I am afraid to upset people. That is not leadership. That is paralysis.
You cannot genuinely believe in everything. If you do, you believe in nothing. A friend to everyone is a friend to no one, and in politics, that mindset guarantees the status quo.
**Compromise is not a starting position. Compromise is an outcome.**
Compromise emerges after people with real convictions fight for what they believe is right. In Congress, in statehouses, and in city councils, compromise happens because people refuse to move unless they get something meaningful. It is forged through tension, not politeness.
Starting from the middle is not wisdom.
I fell into this trap myself early on. When you first run for office, you worry about alienating voters. You worry about sounding too strong. You worry about being labeled. So you soften your language. You talk about unity. You talk about finding common ground. You tell yourself it sounds responsible.
What it really signals is that you have not done the hard work of figuring out what you actually stand for.
Pick any serious issue and try to legislate from a place of deliberate vagueness. You end up pleasing no one, changing nothing, and preserving a system everyone claims to hate. That is how we get endless gridlock paired with endless campaigning.
Understanding opposing arguments is not weakness. Changing your mind when evidence demands it is not weakness. Running for office without firm convictions is. Oh... being "against the other person" is not a conviction.
There is also a difference between two kinds of centrists. One kind looks at the best ideas on the left and the right and deliberately chooses policies that work, even if that angers partisans. That takes courage. The other kind avoids conflict entirely. They call themselves independent but cannot articulate a position without retreating into platitudes. They are easily swayed because they never anchored themselves to anything solid.
Those are not the people you want representing you when decisions actually matter.
Leadership requires accepting that some people will never vote for you. It requires standing up to donors, party structures, and loud minorities. It requires the courage to say, "This is what I believe, and this is what I will fight for," even when it costs you politically.
Being nice to everyone is not political skill. Avoiding hard stances is not strategy. Shaking hands and kissing babies without being able to clearly articulate your positions is not savvy. Voters see through it, even if they cannot always explain why.
If you want to hold office, you owe people clarity. You owe them conviction. You owe them the honesty to say what you believe and the backbone to defend it. Shit... you owe it to yourself.
Exploration belongs in classrooms and conversations. Governing requires decisions.
If you cannot pick a position and stand on it, you are not ready to represent anyone.
_Join other**ANGRY DEMOCRATS** by support Matt's work. Subscribe and Share! _

clean_body: Representation starts with conviction. | | ---|---|--- | | | Forwarded this email? ? for more ? We need to find other ANGRY DEMOCRATS. Please consider sharing and subscribing to fuel the ANGER for change. Representation starts with conviction. | | | Dec 19 | | | | | | | | | | | | The Myth of Compromise | | ---|---|--- One of the most popular lines candidates use when running for office is "I want to work across the aisle" or "I want to compromise." "I want to bring both sides together." It sounds reasonable. It sounds mature. And in practice, it is often a trap for weakness and failure. I say that as someone who loves debate. I studied politics. I seriously considered law school. I enjoy arguments for their own sake. I consume right-wing media, left-wing media, centrist commentary, and everything in between because I believe intellectual curiosity makes you sharper, more empathetic, and better informed. Exploring ideas, morals, ethics, and ideology is healthy. It makes you smarter. But there is a massive difference between intellectual exploration and governing. A representative is not elected to endlessly explore ideas. A representative is elected to pick a position, fight for it, and deliver results for their constituents. When a candidate runs on "seeing both sides" as their core message, what I hear is this: I do not believe strongly in anything, and I am afraid to upset people. That is not leadership. That is paralysis. You cannot genuinely believe in everything. If you do, you believe in nothing. A friend to everyone is a friend to no one, and in politics, that mindset guarantees the status quo. Compromise is not a starting position. Compromise is an outcome. Compromise emerges after people with real convictions fight for what they believe is right. In Congress, in statehouses, and in city councils, compromise happens because people refuse to move unless they get something meaningful. It is forged through tension, not politeness. Starting from the middle is not wisdom. I fell into this trap myself early on. When you first run for office, you worry about alienating voters. You worry about sounding too strong. You worry about being labeled. So you soften your language. You talk about unity. You talk about finding common ground. You tell yourself it sounds responsible. What it really signals is that you have not done the hard work of figuring out what you actually stand for. Pick any serious issue and try to legislate from a place of deliberate vagueness. You end up pleasing no one, changing nothing, and preserving a system everyone claims to hate. That is how we get endless gridlock paired with endless campaigning. Understanding opposing arguments is not weakness. Changing your mind when evidence demands it is not weakness. Running for office without firm convictions is. Oh... being "against the other person" is not a conviction. There is also a difference between two kinds of centrists. One kind looks at the best ideas on the left and the right and deliberately chooses policies that work, even if that angers partisans. That takes courage. The other kind avoids conflict entirely. They call themselves independent but cannot articulate a position without retreating into platitudes. They are easily swayed because they never anchored themselves to anything solid. Those are not the people you want representing you when decisions actually matter. Leadership requires accepting that some people will never vote for you. It requires standing up to donors, party structures, and loud minorities. It requires the courage to say, "This is what I believe, and this is what I will fight for," even when it costs you politically. Being nice to everyone is not political skill. Avoiding hard stances is not strategy. Shaking hands and kissing babies without being able to clearly articulate your positions is not savvy. Voters see through it, even if they cannot always explain why. If you want to hold office, you owe people clarity. You owe them conviction. You owe them the honesty to say what you believe and the backbone to defend it. Shit... you owe it to yourself. Exploration belongs in classrooms and conversations. Governing requires decisions. If you cannot pick a position and stand on it, you are not ready to represent anyone. Join otherANGRY DEMOCRATS** by support Matt's work. Subscribe and Share! | | | | | | | (C) 2025 Matt Diemer 548 Market Street PMB 72296, San Francisco, CA 94104 ? ?

body:
The suspect in the Dec. 13 Brown University mass shooting on the campus of Brown University has been found dead in a storage unit in Salem, New Hampshire, police said last night.

| | |
---
| [](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkb2u2zAMhZ9G3mJI1I-dwUMBI9vt2lFgLDpmIUuGpCS4b1-4zRt0_fCB4Dnn15f_Qo6cHj9xp6mt2Sd610itUfH3kt-pbjk3To_6rActLRCG5kGCvSi4qKsPyPHbmy5MKxGQ7mhSg3PKwHg13TZZuTq7kHOW7B2tDUtY5aLwPoLVTg0dT-c1BeqqlDIWemk0OnkPjgYio0ZhJAVuufS_M6e20Rq_84tKv-S9i9PW2lGF_iHgJuDG6UW19QUDp0axRz65vmWhZ2ONALccQs9WwiDAodCztgZOzEHoGdSgBbj9VJwAd7qrAPdsu6_5WRYSei6Ey3b5fOF35OTrkVPN5WMuuB_IjyT0fGBpiUrd-FCjvGir_b_enokbemNGZ50ctBm6MoXjzTFydYMw8rEjx78Z238N85rgTwAAAP__t6Scgw) | |
# Friday, December 19, 2025
---|---
**Good Morning!** On this day in 1843, [_A Christmas Carol_](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJyk0DFugzAUANDTmC3I_tgGBg9VI1I1SiOlQ9XJAvwJrhwc4U8ot6_aK_QCb3gfJ3tqffDT9a29oaEh2gnXFJAIZ9vNcZ3SGCP56ZqWdMeeHLaOLHBQOwE7UVvX-rBZmTkzIAIWGRpRai0kVLXMRiMUV6qssUPg4FACCoCq6oceFSiNmTe_mgBRCyGkgpzLotW8cxpLRCkqJjk6T3HOv6KfaMQhbPGBc97HWxbMSHRPrHhi0DBotrjQknfIoHk-HOapj3I8rqxokmfF3h5fL_HzfX85bPrl_H3OZuPuqw_BJ10yya-31oc_l_6V8TDwEwAA__8Iy3I5) was first published. The first edition sold out by Christmas Eve, and by 1844, the novella had gone through 13 printings and remains a robust seller more than 175 years later. **Late-breaking news:** The suspect in the Dec. 13 Brown University mass shooting has been found dead in a storage unit in Salem, New Hampshire, police said last night. He was identified as 48-year-old Claudio Manuel Neves Valente, a former Brown student who officials also believe to be responsible for the shooting death of an MIT professor two days later. The investigation is continuing. [Here are updates](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJyk0D1uhDAUBODTmM7INuavcJGGbtOmRDZ-LC8xNsIPELePdotcIPVIo_nm6zE-LAaMz0-7gqE5jRGuHIAI9tHt6Yp5SYkwPvORN5jIg_U0KqFqLhWX_egthnvUhTczgIKqACPbppFadb0uFtOpSorZib4VtfBeO18LJ9qpm_u2aitfoHm1SSV7KaWuVSl0ZRvhfAMtgJYd0wI8UtrL74SRFpjDnU7YyymtRTAL0ZZZ9cHUwNRwXVc5ufxSvHKmhoAn8GPzliAzNbxR_Ih4wp6Rbr4i8dXG5YjE_7B8tTd3wAPGH_BMDcVu_HZhCJiblmnxXC2G9wL6122nUb8BAAD__3gqg_c). Today's sponsor, [RAD Intel](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkb2u2zAMhZ9G3mJI1I-dwUMBI9vt2lFgLDpmIUuGpCS4b1-4zRt0_fCB4Dnn15f_Qo6cHj9xp6mt2Sd610itUfH3kt-pbjk3To_6rActLRCG5kGCvSi4qKsPyPHbmy5MKxGQ7mhSg3PKwHg13TZZuTq7kHOW7B2tDUtY5aLwPoLVTg0dT-c1BeqqlDIWemk0OnkPjgYio0ZhJAVuufS_M6e20Rq_84tKv-S9i9PW2lGF_iHgJuDG6UW19QUDp0axRz65vmWhZ2ONALccQs9WwiDAodCztgZOzEHoGdSgBbj9VJwAd7qrAPdsu6_5WRYSei6Ey3b5fOF35OTrkVPN5WMuuB_IjyT0fGBpiUrd-FCjvGir_b_enokbemNGZ50ctBm6MoXjzTFydYMw8rEjx78Z238N85rgTwAAAP__t6Scgw), is a rare private AI company already generating real revenue and attracting thousands of early investors--offering everyday readers a chance to look at what could be the next wave of AI before Wall Street fully catches on.
|
---
## TikTok Signs US Sale Deal
Chinese TikTok owner ByteDance signed [binding agreements Thursday](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJyk0LGO2zAQBNCvobo1RIqWrIKFcwGLAJfmipQCRa5kxhRX4NIW_PeB8wtXDzCYeX8-p08XU8zrb7ehqQtNGQ9OWCuWaS50ZL4R1ZhXfvCOvgZ0oU6qVWeQCuQ4BRfTa9JNMAuiwq5BI4e-l1pdRt3czOj9rLt5CHg-exylv1zmdhkHqTG4tu-aaN5tUslRSqnP6tTqzvXtHHocELW8CN1iiJXK6S_FXG-4pBc9sZw8bU0yt1p3Ft1VKCuUPY7jtHF-Z0JZzPBgoexGGV9C2fc5oSx7oh1qvFe6A8c1MwR0CRYqwC4h0AIPhkeOFdxSscALXWFIlFdgtzqhrCtwvcqvj4_7L9FZ8jGI7uePtyQe_IVlb4oJ-xFTitwPQrfr5mL6v7l-C_pp1L8AAAD__2d_lC8) to sell over 80% of TikTok's U.S. assets to American investors, ending years of fighting over China's influence on the popular social media platform. Under the agreements, Oracle, Silver Lake, and Abu Dhabi's MGX will collectively own 45% of the new TikTok joint venture, with existing ByteDance investors keeping about 30% and ByteDance retaining nearly 20%. The deal, set to close Jan. 22, requires the U.S. owners to [retrain TikTok's algorithm](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkLGOszAQhJ_GdEZ4cWwoXPwNXf72SmTwAntx7Ahvgnj7E_cK14000oy-7-s-3j1FSut__0THSx4THiUiM-7jtOcjlS1nprSWd3nhzAF94BEauEkFUvVj8BTPUVfBLYiAbYVOWWOUhq7X1ebMssy2s3qeW-t1A6o12qrWdnoKXvW6InetKVC9UkrfoG50600zBYMWUatO6AYDcd7r70yJN1zimT-413N-VtFtzK8i2n8CBgHDcRz1PJWL4uoFDFcUMDA9OD9koTUVGdBHueRdFh9RcpbvIjEx8SlgqHYXXgfFSMVYoZv16Sn-nvGfDH0c_AQAAP__WKF7LQ) using only American user data to prevent outside manipulation by China. The sale allows 170 million U.S. users to continue scrolling while addressing concerns that first emerged when President Trump attempted to ban the app in 2020.
## 'Patriot Games' Announced For America 250
The Trump administration on Thursday announced plans for a new national high school sports competition called the ["Patriot Games" as part](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJyk0DGO6yAQgOHT2J0jZgLYLiheky6v3dIiZrBZYUAwiZXbr7JX2PqXvuL_ui93G2JI2397kGGfl0Rni8RMdXnUfKa258whbe3ZCq3syDpeUKAaAAeYF2dDfC-yd8YTIV17MjBqDRKnWfa7Uco74YDmhx5x8uCV0ujXVUoNQom5D-ajAcIMAFLhRcir1eLhNI1EEqZOCnKBc71855B4Jx_f-UX1suajj2ZnLq27_uvw1uGtUC6RPqnDG9fnUQabUn6mldpQLNeQedjsQW0AmEaUiH01rpwhxtD02EmxHTbEX5z_dORl8CcAAP__wfN05g) of the country’s 250th anniversary celebration in 2026. The four-day event, expected in the fall, would bring one male and one female student-athlete from every state and U.S. territory to Washington, D.C. Watch [President Trump's announcement here](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJyk0LFugzAQxvGnMVsQdxgTBg-VIqQOqdSp3Sw7PoJbB0f4Eos-fUVfoev3Sb_h_3E2ZxtiWK5v9kaap2QWKjkSM63GrakseU6Jw3LNj3ynC3uyng022B0ADzAYb0PcjKy8noiQ2oo09EqBxOMgq1lPvetUJ3EYOuWcOsp2cOD6qbOeEHqogt41QBgAQHZYN7K1qnFeUU8k4ShkQz5wWuuvFBaeaYpbetJaX9KtinpmvmfRvggcBY6llHpLD3442v99sXyZRTs-RXv6ydv36X18_bSpWrW_lxBjyKoXsrnebIh_JP-rw1PjbwAAAP__Q1BxjA). The Patriot Games are part of a [broader America 250 slate of commemorative events](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkDGOpDAQAF9jsh7hxuPZCQguIdtLL0SN3UCfjI3snkH7-9PeFzYvlVT153P-JEmSt9908KhrmTNfLbEq13mp5cptL0Ulb-3VTg4amaLO2OMdLIJ9zpEkfc2ui-PKjDx0PNqH99bhx9N1-ziQi54sLxycD9Fb9ItlF_zgsae4djJ-2yzap7XW3fHWu4F8v0TPD2ZnP4zrOYqWevtbJOvOa_oqb663UI4ujbvq2czwy-BkcLqu65aXcFHbJW9a8jdlcOKsXJUkH5zV4KQ7Qwuc2eB0klYpChsd3CCTSsmU4KCUoCkpw0pS4dpFGfbyagyvNcAq264QA9DBVQIB3ns4E-UGld9MiaPByfWDxWEwOHV1jOclKUnzD-P67SBJ_yP0R-ffI_4LAAD__77umxw) that also includes a UFC fight at the White House on Flag Day, a multi-week Great American State Fair on the National Mall, and expanded July 4 celebrations with military flyovers and fireworks. The administration says more details on events and scheduling will be released as planning continues. [Visit the official website here](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykzzFu7CAQgOHTQLcWjDFrFxSv2W5fmxJhGNsTYWPBZK29fZRcIQf4f-n7ePpnoEzH-j_s6Hgp_sCrZWTG6udarqNtpTAda_tqJ0ZOGBJ7UDDcNNz05FOg_PZGJrcgAvYSnb5bqw2Mk5GbA2MtxDiGOSwm4jhBr8yw4NhbHWZASe7npkFPWmszQKdMH6yak8U7otGjMAoTcandZ6GDN1zyu7ywdrHsMruN-Wyi_yfgIeARdqwUAwyqK3UV8JDVpfOinKnZuzBq3QPl35T_5H05-A4AAP__Mh1o-A).
## College Football Playoffs Start Tonight
The 12-team College Football Playoff kicks off this weekend [with four first-round, win-or-go-home](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkL2O5CAQhJ8GZ1jQZv0TEFzibC-90MJ0Y3PbYyxgx5q3P_mkeYKNq1Sq7_vzuXy6yPHYfrsH2RrSctBVmGqlvKw5XUfZU6rx2Mp3OclXJId1AQUfUoPU04Iu8msxDdpABNQ1ZPXQ99rAOJlmt4gKKYwddtiPGieFHlS_Tm5UyriwNtHeaxr0pLU2H9Aq07lerdjTQGT0KIwijDXl9m-KR90p8Cs9Kbc-PRq2e61nEd0vAbOA-bqu9vDO3aGA-aYRMIeU6uqYBcwu1-iZBMxvCDUImH1ipo3kuylPdq8Uglyz819UZfE74TeTzO74un3IQoSlyRbPKzLH0g_CqO3hIv9_Vn-k82nhXwAAAP__l-CLIw) games, starting with tonight’s matchup between No. 9 Alabama and No. 8 Oklahoma and rolling through to a tripleheader tomorrow. Tomorrow’s midday slate opens with No. 10 Miami taking on No. 7 Texas A&M. The Aggies have been monitoring the health of superstar running back Le’Veon Moss all week as he pushes to return after missing the last six games with an [ankle injury](https://email.editor.jointheflyover.com/c/eJykkDHPmzAURX8NbI7ww7GTgaEL29e1I3rYF3DlYGq_JM2_r5Kp-7eeIx3p3l9f0xfHFPf1J98wyJKnHc-aIIIyzSU_97rlLHFf670e8BLAQSbq6Kw0KX2dAsf0mkwbhgUg9C0G7azVhi5X024DelgP8MU721Hou7k_u66_LLP1fPZo4_CuadJXrbU506kzPdtuDhYOMPrSmA4hSi6n3znusmFJr_xAOfl8a9OwiRy16X80NDY0knH1yEXq2zY0-pwSVjQ0Cv5yVfyGXCT69D9UZVYJD-Rd3XKtKsuGonhdI9SR-IVSVdzVnzuqxLyr45OIBwvCW1ThIiov6ijsJXoouaMGfimy1lHnnG5obMsQjmdMKVbrGtOtN47pM0O-9f1joH8BAAD__1c1m8Q). The TNT cable network then takes over with a double feature full of upset potential: No. 11 Tulane visits No. 6 Ole Miss in the afternoon, then No. 12 James Madison heads to No. 5 Oregon in the evening. That’s two Group-of-Five champions walking into two of the loudest stadiums in the sport with a shot to flip the bracket in one night. Tonight’s opening game between Alabama and Oklahoma is a rematch of the nailbiter from Nov. 15, which the Sooners won 23-21. Kickoff is set for 8 p.m. ET on ABC and ESPN.

clean_body: The suspect in the Dec. 13 Brown University mass shooting on the campus of Brown University has been found dead in a storage unit in Salem, New Hampshire, police said last night. | | | | | | Friday, December 19, 2025 ---|--- Good Morning! On this day in 1843, was first published. The first edition sold out by Christmas Eve, and by 1844, the novella had gone through 13 printings and remains a robust seller more than 175 years later. Late-breaking news: The suspect in the Dec. 13 Brown University mass shooting has been found dead in a storage unit in Salem, New Hampshire, police said last night. He was identified as 48-year-old Claudio Manuel Neves Valente, a former Brown student who officials also believe to be responsible for the shooting death of an MIT professor two days later. The investigation is continuing. . Today's sponsor, , is a rare private AI company already generating real revenue and attracting thousands of early investors--offering everyday readers a chance to look at what could be the next wave of AI before Wall Street fully catches on. | TikTok Signs US Sale Deal Chinese TikTok owner ByteDance signed to sell over 80% of TikTok's U.S. assets to American investors, ending years of fighting over China's influence on the popular social media platform. Under the agreements, Oracle, Silver Lake, and Abu Dhabi's MGX will collectively own 45% of the new TikTok joint venture, with existing ByteDance investors keeping about 30% and ByteDance retaining nearly 20%. The deal, set to close Jan. 22, requires the U.S. owners to using only American user data to prevent outside manipulation by China. The sale allows 170 million U.S. users to continue scrolling while addressing concerns that first emerged when President Trump attempted to ban the app in 2020. 'Patriot Games' Announced For America 250 The Trump administration on Thursday announced plans for a new national high school sports competition called the of the country’s 250th anniversary celebration in 2026. The four-day event, expected in the fall, would bring one male and one female student-athlete from every state and U.S. territory to Washington, D.C. Watch . The Patriot Games are part of a that also includes a UFC fight at the White House on Flag Day, a multi-week Great American State Fair on the National Mall, and expanded July 4 celebrations with military flyovers and fireworks. The administration says more details on events and scheduling will be released as planning continues. . College Football Playoffs Start Tonight The 12-team College Football Playoff kicks off this weekend games, starting with tonight’s matchup between No. 9 Alabama and No. 8 Oklahoma and rolling through to a tripleheader tomorrow. Tomorrow’s midday slate opens with No. 10 Miami taking on No. 7 Texas A&M. The Aggies have been monitoring the health of superstar running back Le’Veon Moss all week as he pushes to return after missing the last six games with an . The TNT cable network then takes over with a double feature full of upset potential: No. 11 Tulane visits No. 6 Ole Miss in the afternoon, then No. 12 James Madison heads to No. 5 Oregon in the evening. That’s two Group-of-Five champions walking into two of the loudest stadiums in the sport with a shot to flip the bracket in one night. Tonight’s opening game between Alabama and Oklahoma is a rematch of the nailbiter from Nov. 15, which the Sooners won 23-21. Kickoff is set for 8 p.m. ET on ABC and ESPN. A 16-year-old student at Baytown's Ross S. Sterling High School was [fatally stabbed](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkL3S2yAQRZ8GdWhgAf0UFGnonDalBgESTDB4xNqy3z4j5xG-Zqude-85f27Lzaacyv7b3oPG91aXEs6WA2I4luZirbmhXddU9ti2WnG1ObsFGCjKgfJ58TblzyI7r1fmZ94Fzcdh4FKNauqiVoqPsIrNCjuIwUtw0s3ehwlmsc1CdUlfYRz4zIGBkD0w5dU4CRnYJOQARLLgE9ajxxgwvG3b8qe-wtG7eu-yjoiPRsQvAoaAOc-zdzm5vxDrs2Et1xcBc2ERMLk6mwmYq5OA4XCdkYBp-PShIP3SBk8t0pj2SP87oKnQ1X6wnoX655HKTre0RyRgukP7x5lyTm0YiWT73ab8nYY_E_rS8C8AAP__4XWJbA) by another student with a pair of scissors on Wednesday. # Friday, December 19, 2025
---|---
**Good Morning!** On this day in 1842, the [Somervell expedition](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0EGOrCAQgOHT4K4NlCi6YPE27vptZ2lASyFTUh0gbfftJz1XmAv8X_J_3Ze7ixTT8d-daOtr5yXhVQhrxbyUNTBTqc77mI5QdubqHdG6gIT-puCmpmVzkd6Lbjbr5TapBq0yw6B0b_qxCXYcTed36Z1WADAq75Uxblr3VU27kV0T7SemQE0KJHS6BdlvvRk7jXLs9ABCS9xi5dzWgBVfruz05ifmduWzIRtqfRTR_RMwC5iv62prCY4TxYQt50PAHFzaPPO3gBlTzRGLgLnwifmJRDd8PT5C5NRkuz2uSBTLYISWx-ki_Tr1b3eeFn4CAAD__61Uekw) disbanded near Laredo. Sam Houston authorized the expedition in retaliation for several Mexican incursions into Texas. Over 300 men who participated in the effort refused to go home and continued into Mexico with the ill-fated Mier expedition. A new hot chicken franchise is coming to Texas next year. Check out today's _Business_ section for the [details](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0L2SrCAUBOCn0QwLjiAaENzEbG66ocXPUalFmAJmdPbpt2ZeYfOur7r767bctA8-bv_1gapea1oiniVgrZiXYveUQqnaGB-3vawpVaNDsAtQEIQBYdPitA-vhbdOGeom1qJichgYF1KM7a6MGZy0dhUj1ZNcuXEoeiqpkVKIyZrWqzfGgE0MKPS8AyqckGPPkY49H6DhFJ2vKXd1x4qXLmt4pSfmzqajDWqv9V6a_l8DcwPzeZ5dxlL1I-tY32PesQZmHbf8Inb39vuH4HXX0RUS8SQHXt4m8pEJCPKIvpI162h3X5A41IEwoBxEA3OblbufPgRfBtlwuh3ah0-R-rf7ngp-AwAA__-R9YV7). As you finish your holiday shopping, today’s sponsor, [Brad's Deals](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkMGOpCAQhp8Gb3agEGwPHDbpeJu97pEUUCoJiiP0OP32G3f7DeZa9efL_39_PuwHxhS3-TeuZOr3lO1GZ0lUKx22-CXnVCo6F7d5KVPO1WFK3gIH1QpoxWADxvSyXROM42EQDRnRay061at7sxhCPoVpkv3dOSU9BA9ae049aOUH5ZpoLpgAMQjgILsbcBVUf5cd8bvsNLCOU4g1H7e6UKVvLFN65S86bj6vTTJLrXth8heDkcF4nufNHRhKIEzlijAY0X8yGBNuIW4zk-Ozrrbk5-GJyUdd6A1sQ0UG-vquFOJzZfIRYtkTvt5nj-uOcd6YfMy00YGpRf_530R77WCgvY2ByYfgA6jmMGE_Y0qx6J51fF4xpn-9689sfxn4GwAA__9rtJOX), offers a free, personalized newsletter with last-chance sales and handpicked deals from brands you already love.

## Baytown Student Fatally Stabbed
A 16-year-old student at Baytown's Ross S. Sterling High School was [fatally stabbed](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkL3S2yAQRZ8GdWhgAf0UFGnonDalBgESTDB4xNqy3z4j5xG-Zqude-85f27Lzaacyv7b3oPG91aXEs6WA2I4luZirbmhXddU9ti2WnG1ObsFGCjKgfJ58TblzyI7r1fmZ94Fzcdh4FKNauqiVoqPsIrNCjuIwUtw0s3ehwlmsc1CdUlfYRz4zIGBkD0w5dU4CRnYJOQARLLgE9ajxxgwvG3b8qe-wtG7eu-yjoiPRsQvAoaAOc-zdzm5vxDrs2Et1xcBc2ERMLk6mwmYq5OA4XCdkYBp-PShIP3SBk8t0pj2SP87oKnQ1X6wnoX655HKTre0RyRgukP7x5lyTm0YiWT73ab8nYY_E_rS8C8AAP__4XWJbA) by another student with a pair of scissors on Wednesday. The victim has been identified as Andrew Meismer, while the alleged perpetrator is Aundre Matthews, 18. The school was placed on a temporary hold as Meismer was transported to a hospital via Life Flight. Classes [resumed](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0LGOpDAQBNCvMZlHdttgCBxcQraXXoiM3YB1BiO6d2f5-9PMfcLGJZXq1Z-P6SPkko_1d9jR8_dSpwOfVJAZr4niVmshDvOcj3WjpVaeQylxAgWt1CD1MKWQyz3ZJvlZpUE36LXrOm1b1_bN5s2srFPJDCGlmJxbjDWpx1YNugsGhib7V5kGPWhQYOwDVJta1xuLqje2A2EVpsz1evCGjN-BlnLXL7wese5N8RvzScL8EjAKGMMctXklAsazEgsYifF6GeWW103-R0niz4QHyzncXJ-HfCsxyRj285PkWiuhjBfiXxkzJUnhJgGj7mFoTecEjM3l0_nMpWTqnLBq3UMu7038sye_PPwLAAD__wSgh94) after the suspect was apprehended, and students returned to school yesterday "with a full crisis response team in place." Families have criticized the administration for poor communication, continuing classes in the wake of the violence, and allowing Matthews to attend the school despite his alleged disciplinary history. ## Texas Targets Abortion Pill in Federal Lawsuit
Texas and Florida are challenging the widespread [availability of mifepristone](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0L2OpDAQBOCngcwj_MdP4OASsr30QtTYDbS2wSO7b9l9-9PMK1xe-lRVfz6WDyCma_8NJwb53vJy4V0ZRbAsNR45cxVYV7r2o245ywrMcTGd8UobpaclAfHP4toU1i5NusWgh77Xzg9-bI_gB91PI9g-9k6PfkyjHaxBP0WLawTbUnhh2uhJm85Y9zCdT34YrcNutK43jeswkeTykAMFv6Fu_JO_sDxiPlsOh8izNvZXY-bGzPd9P14TtpJPObAKCNZXsjEzFKHI2JiZ4a5_SdSbUxvnQgkU7FUJfGJVcGU5sKhYIH4qppOErl3BmotQvtSTmBXEiLW2JaTnTcxU-6Fx3X4C8bua_N-hX8H8CwAA__9RC43K) in a 120-page lawsuit that seeks to invalidate the FDA's approval of the abortion drug and guidelines that dramatically expanded access to it. Since Roe v. Wade was overturned in 2022, a drug cocktail of mifepristone and misoprostol has become the [most common method](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkLuu6yAURL8Gd0SwAT8Kitu4y21PaW0MxEg7xgLy-vsj5xdOPZo1mvVzXa6YKO23_3gPtr1jXvbwqhRaC2Wp65Yz1YbOpf221Zhzc0i0LiDAcAlcTovHRJ9Fd9464SfZBSuHvpfaDGbsNisHo1yA0SgdjRj6KSrv-zhgP-EIk-qSPWES5CRBgNIXEMabYVQ6iFHpHpgWwaeWy6VtoYU31kif_AzlsuZ7R3Zr7ahM_WMwM5hXV_FRW9rPlMF83mEwU16RGMzfPo-US_LI6yPw6JGfNI4ul5byzo9ExPE4Sn4idcX645WIUu0HpsXtjom-y-1vvp4WfgMAAP__-_OANg) of terminating a pregnancy in the U.S. Abortion advocates insist there are few risks for women who take mifepristone, while opponents argue [the drug's FDA label](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJyk0DGO6yAQgOHT4C4WjME4BcVrXL3slltaAwM20iREho2T26-yV9gL_J_0f12WC2bOt_UDr9G1ZyrLLR6VY2txX2rYSuHa0Pt8W7eaSmkemcMCEsxJwUmdF8LMr0V35Lyks-qiU3YclTbWTN3mTJRDQjPigGQ1eAve0hAgxUCkPHbZvWMK1FmBhEH3IA0ZOw06ymnQIwgtI-VW9r5tscUn1sSv8oh7H8q1Y7e1dq9i-CdgFjAfx9FjCLFWwoZ9IuzX8hAw0_69VmyJcKESqoCZ0UcWML9ZAbMEOU72c8-rqhLMf8_9nVK3O7ofmTnX0Qot1ytm_pXb3349HPwEAAD___bwfio) indicates 2.9% to 4.6% of women who take it could require emergency care. Since the drug’s approval in 2000, the restriction limiting its use to seven weeks of gestation has been increased to 10, and it has been approved for distribution through the mail—a violation of the Comstock Act of 1873, [according to the lawsuit](https://email.editor.thetexasflyover.com/c/eJykkL1utDAQAJ_GdEb2Yv4KF19Dd1-bEhl2DVYMPtl7x93bR0keIf1oNJqP23xzIYZz--8OsvzyaT7pKpGYKc9l3VOKhd2yhHPbi0-JFxfjOoOCVmqQepzRhfieTYV2UTjqiqzuu06btm-HareESjtF66g8KW1aXKHVw4jeo-9d11XBfss06FGDgsbUoFps-6ExpIbGdCCMIgyccs07Mb1c8fGdnpTrNR1VtDvzvYjmn4BJwHRdV72mR-YYCtP5SwmYMK2fxAKmHpSGcTACJi1g4p1kYcckk5c-phzQyad8FOlTQulOlJgfm3R4hDMUzo5DOgVMVbZ4v0KMoXS9MGo7XIg_Sfy3kU8LXwEAAP__0t6H6Q).

Busy day? Listen to our daily 15-minute podcast while commuting, cooking, or walking the dog—your Flyover fix, anywhere. | | Baytown Student Fatally Stabbed A 16-year-old student at Baytown's Ross S. Sterling High School was by another student with a pair of scissors on Wednesday. The victim has been identified as Andrew Meismer, while the alleged perpetrator is Aundre Matthews, 18. The school was placed on a temporary hold as Meismer was transported to a hospital via Life Flight. Classes after the suspect was apprehended, and students returned to school yesterday "with a full crisis response team in place." Families have criticized the administration for poor communication, continuing classes in the wake of the violence, and allowing Matthews to attend the school despite his alleged disciplinary history. A group of at least 25 students outside the school yesterday morning. One of them held a sign that read, "How many warnings is enough? " "It was really scary for a lot of us ," , a student who knew Meismer. Texas Targets Abortion Pill in Federal Lawsuit Texas and Florida are challenging the widespread in a 120-page lawsuit that seeks to invalidate the FDA's approval of the abortion drug and guidelines that dramatically expanded access to it. Since Roe v. Wade was overturned in 2022, a drug cocktail of mifepristone and misoprostol has become the of terminating a pregnancy in the U.S. Abortion advocates insist there are few risks for women who take mifepristone, while opponents argue indicates 2.9% to 4.6% of women who take it could require emergency care. Since the drug’s approval in 2000, the restriction limiting its use to seven weeks of gestation has been increased to 10, and it has been approved for distribution through the mail—a violation of the Comstock Act of 1873, . High School Football State Title Games Kick Off This is the last week of Texas high school football, with 12 state title games being played at Arlington's AT&T Stadium. body:
“AR will be as big as smartphones — it’s a question of who builds the ecosystem.”| | | [](https://links.team.happyretirementreport.com/u/click?_t=f01b136938e2492c8217fbace1bf2931&_m=4e0c8f30dce7476fb379fe43e72568a0&_e=uboUr4m4QvalGuDVzr5gwT04ych5oiCZ4KTIh0XdWC2P4Rr8G1_eN7o-7D2zezoOKjw1u-QzPrBq-qpIEPS1M7WlAWHHKblsB3NGCPstFBby8As_kP_s7Jc7cHt6Sp7OU4743zv4JYPx41qIssEn3xg9mBO6Ybi6b-gTPh3ALq5Y5Cz29c-kg4qUW9rJE1U1LVN4lEEhgLAxjXa92XqYHnZUVS8wmn1INoKiohsMKWDPXVJyXhggjK_CkecDH233APMzrDsrWYJ9mie1PIk_6fS2F5NOsNGndfl8F3g54BOBSq-LBRtzg2rfHAPeiwK9KCpLqLg6tDNMJvjjhA5q_Q%3D%3D)
---
King was born in Portland, Maine, on September 21, 1947. His father, Donald Ed King, a travelling vacuum salesman after returning from World War II,[10] was born in Indiana with the sur Pollock, changing it to King as an adult.[11][12][13] King's mother was Nellie Ruth King (née Pillsbury).[13] His parents were married in Scarborough, Maine on July 23, 1939.[14] Shortly afterwards, they lived with Donald's family in Chicago before moving to Croton-on-Hudson, York.[15] King's parents returned to Maine towards the end of World War II, living in a modest house in Scarborough. When King was two, his father left the family. His mother raised him and his older brother David by herself, sometimes under strain. They moved from Scarborough and depended on relatives in Chicago; Croton-on-Hudson; West De Pere, Wisconsin; Fort Wayne, Indiana; Malden, Massachusetts; and Stratford, Connecticut.[15][17] When King was 11, his family moved to Durham, Maine, where his mother cared for her parents until their deaths. She then became a caregiver in a local residential facility for the menty chenged.[1] King was raised Methodist,[18][19] but lost his belief in organized religion while in high school. While no longer religious, he says he chooses to believe in the existence of God.[20] As a child, King apparently witnessed one of his friends being struck and killed by a train, though he has no memory of the event. His family told him that after leaving to play with the boy, King returned speechless and seemingly in shock. later did the family learn of the death. Some commentators have suggested that this event may have psychologicy inspired some of King's darker works,[21] but King makes no mention of it in his memoir On Writing (2000). He related in detail his primary inspiration for writing horror fiction in his non-fiction Danse Macabre (1981), in a chapter titled "An Annoying Autobiographical Pause". He compared his uncle's dowsing for water using the bough of an apple branch with the sudden realization of what he wanted to do for a living. That inspiration occurred while browsing through an attic with his elder brother, when King uncovered a paperback version of an H. P. Lovecraft collection of short stories he remembers as The Lurker in the Shadows, that had belonged to his father. King told Barnes & Noble Studios in a 2009 interview, "I k that I'd found when I read that book."[22] King attended Durham Elementary School and graduated from Lisbon High School (Maine) in Lisbon Fs, Maine, in 1966.[23] He displayed an early interest in horror as an avid reader of EC horror comics, including Tales from the Crypt, and he later paid tribute to the comics in his screenplay for Creepshow. He began writing for fun while in school, contributing articles to Dave's Rag, the spaper his brother published with a mimeograph machine, and later began selling stories to his friends based on movies he had seen. (He was forced to return the when it was discovered by his teachers.) The first of his stories to be independently published was "I Was a Teenage Grave Robber", which was serialized over four issues (three published and one unpublished) of a fanzine, Comics Review, in 1965. It was republished the follog year in revised, as "In a Half-World of Terror", in another fanzine, Stories of Suspense, edited by Marv Wolfman.[24] As a tееn, King also a Scholastic Art and Writing Award.[25] King entered the University of Maine in 1966, and graduated in 1970 with a Bachelor of Arts in English.[26] That year, his daughter Naomi Rachel was born. He wrote a column, Steve King's Garbage Truck, for the student spaper, The Maine Campus, and participated in a writing workshop organized by Burton Hatlen.[27] King held a variety of jobs to pay for his studies, including as a janitor, a gas-station attendant, and an industrial laundry worker. He met his, fellow student Tabitha Spruce, at the university's Raymond H. Fogler Library after one of Professor Hatlen's workshops; they wed in 1971.[27] Career Beginnings In 1971, King worked as a teacher at Hampden Academy. King sold his first professional short story, "The Glass Floor", to Startling Mystery Stories in 1967.[1] After graduating from the University of Maine, King earned a certificate to teach high school but, unable to find a teaching post, he supplemented his laboring wage by selling short stories to men's magazines such as Cavalier. Many of these early stories were republished in the collection Night Shift. The short story "The Raft" was published in Adam, a men's magazine. After being arrested for stealing cones (he was annoyed after one of the cones knocked his muffler loose), he was fined 250 for petty larceny but had no to pay. However, a then arrived for "The Raft" (then titled "The Float"), and King cashed it to pay the fine.[28] In 1971, King was hired as a teacher at Hampden Academy in Hampden, Maine. He continued to contribute short stories to magazines and worked on ideas for novels.[1] During 1966–1970, he wrote a draft about his dystopian novel ced The Long Walk[29] and the anti-war novel Sword in the Darkness,[30][31] but neither of the works was published at the time; The Long Walk was later released in 1979. Carrie and aftermath In 1973, King's novel, Carrie, was accepted by publishing house, Doubleday. It was King's fourth novel,[32] but the first to be published. He wrote it on his Tabitha's portable typewriter. It began as a short story intended for Cavalier magazine, but King tossed the first three pages in the garbage can.[33] Tabitha recovered the pages and encouraged him to finish the story, saying she would help him with the female perspective; he followed her advice and expanded it into a novel.[34] He said: "I persisted because I was dry and had no better ideas... My considered opinion was that I had written the world's -time loser."[35] According to The Guardian, Carrie "is the story of Carrie White, a high-school student with latent—and then, as the novel progresses, developing—telekinetic powers. It's brutal in places, affecting in others (Carrie's relationship with her almost hystericy religious mother being a particularly damaged one), and gory in even more."[36] When Carrie was chosen for publication, King's was out of service. Doubleday editor William Thompson—who became King's close—sent a telegram to King's house in late March or early April 1973[37] which read: "Carrie Officiy A Doubleday Book. 2,500 Advance Against Royalties. Congrats, Kid – The Future Lies Ahead, Bill."[38] King said he bought a Ford Pinto with the advance.[37] On May 13, 1973, American Library bought the paperback rights for 400,000, which—in accordance with King's contract with Doubleday—was split between them.[39][40] Carrie set King's career in motion and became a significant novel in the horror genre. In 1976, it was made into a successful horror film.[41] King's 'Salem's Lot was published in 1975. In a 1987 issue of The Highway Patrolman magazine, he said, "The story seems sort of down home to me. I have a special cold spot in my heart for it!"[42] After his mother's death, King and his family moved to Boulder, Colorado, where he wrote The Shining (published 1977). The family returned to Auburn, Maine in 1975, where he completed The Stand (published 1978). In 1977, the family, with the addition of Owen Philip, his third and youngest child, traveled briefly to England. They returned to Maine that f, where King began teaching creative writing at the University of Maine.[43] In 1982, King published Different Seasons, a collection of four novellas with a more dramatic bent than the horror fiction for which he is famous.[44] It is notable for having three of its four novellas turned into Hollywood films: Stand by Me (1986) was adapted from The Body;[45] The Shawshank Redemption (1994) was adapted from Rita Hayworth and Shawshank Redemption;[46] and Apt Pupil (1998) was adapted from the novella of the same .[47][48] In 1985, King wrote his first work for the comic book,[49] writing a few pages of the benefit X-Men comic book Heroes for Hope Starring the X-Men. The book, whose were donated to famine relief in Africa, was written by a number of different authors in the comic book field, such as Chris Claremont, Stan Lee, and Alan Moore, as well as authors not primarily associated with comics, such as Harlan Ellison.[50] The follog year, King published It (1986), which was t-selling hardcover novel in the United States that year,[51] and wrote the introduction to Batman No. 400, an anniversary issue where he expressed his preference for the character over Superman.[52][53] The Dark Tower books Main article: The Dark Tower (series) In the late 1970s, King began what became a series of interconnected stories about a lone gunslinger, Roland, who pursues the "Man in Black" in an alternate-reality universe that is a cross between J. R. R. Tolkien's Middle-earth and the American Wild West as depicted by Clint Eastwood and Sergio Leone in their spaghetti Westerns. The first of these stories, The Dark Tower: The Gunslinger, was initiy published in five instments by The Magazine of Fantasy & Science Fiction under the editorship of Edward L. Ferman, from 1977 to 1981. The Gunslinger was continued as an eight-book epic series ced The Dark Tower, whose books King wrote and published infrequently over four decades (1978-2012).[54] Pseudonyms In the late 1970s and early 1980s, King published a handful of short novels—Rage (1977), The Long Walk (1979), Roadwork (1981), The Running Man (1982) and Thinner (1984)—under the pseudonym Richard Bachman. The idea behind this was to test whether he could replicate his again and to ay his fears that his popularity was an accident. An alternate explanation was that publishing standards at the time owed a single book a year.[55] He picked up the from the Canadian hard rock band Bachman–Turner Overdrive, of which he is a fan.[56] Richard Bachman was exposed as King's pseudonym by a persistent Washington, D.C. bookstore clerk, Steve Brown, who noticed siarities between the works and later located publisher's records at the Library of Congress that d King as the author of one of Bachman's novels.[57] This led to a press release heralding Bachman's "death"—supposedly from "cancer of the pseudonym".[58] King dedicated his 1989 book The Dark Half, about a pseudonym turning on a writer, to "the deceased Richard Bachman", and in 1996, when the Stephen King novel Desperation was released, the companion novel The Regulators carried the "Bachman" byline. In 2006, during a press conference in London, King declared that he had discovered another Bachman novel, titled Blaze. It was published on June 12, 2007. In fact, the original manuscript had been held at King's Alma mater, the University of Maine in Orono, for many years and had been covered by numerous King experts. King rewrote the original 1973 manuscript for its publication.[59] King has used other pseudonyms. The short story "The Fifth Quarter" was published under the pseudonym John Swithen (the of a character in the novel Carrie), by Cavalier in April 1972.[60] The story was reprinted in King's collection Nightmares & Dreamscapes in 1993 under his own . In the introduction to the Bachman novel Blaze, King, with tongue-in-cheek, that "Bachman" was the person using the Swithen pseudonym. The "children's book" Charlie the Choo-Choo: From the World of The Dark Tower was published in 2015 under the pseudonym Beryl Evans, who was portrayed by actress ison Davies during a book signing at San Diego Comic-Con,[61] and illustrated by Ned Dameron. It is adapted from a fictional book central to the plot of King's previous novel The Dark Tower III: The Waste Lands.[62] Digital era Stephen King at the Harvard Book Store, June 6, 2005 In 2000, King published online a serialized horror novel, The Plant.[63] At first the public assumed that King had abandoned the project because were unsuccessful, but King later stated that he had simply run out of stories.[64] The unfinished epistolary novel is still available from King's official site, . Also in 2000, he wrote a digital novella, Riding the Bullet, and saying he foresaw e-books becoming 50 of the market "probably by 2013 and maybe by 2012". However, he also stated: "the thing—people tire of the toys quickly."[65] King wrote the first draft of the 2001 novel Dreamcatcher...

clean_body: “AR will be as big as smartphones — it’s a question of who builds the ecosystem.”| | | King was born in Portland, Maine, on September 21, 1947. His father, Donald Ed King, a travelling vacuum salesman after returning from World War II,[10] was born in Indiana with the sur Pollock, changing it to King as an adult.[11][12][13] King's mother was Nellie Ruth King (née Pillsbury).[13] His parents were married in Scarborough, Maine on July 23, 1939.[14] Shortly afterwards, they lived with Donald's family in Chicago before moving to Croton-on-Hudson, York.[15] King's parents returned to Maine towards the end of World War II, living in a modest house in Scarborough. When King was two, his father left the family. His mother raised him and his older brother David by herself, sometimes under strain. They moved from Scarborough and depended on relatives in Chicago; Croton-on-Hudson; West De Pere, Wisconsin; Fort Wayne, Indiana; Malden, Massachusetts; and Stratford, Connecticut.[15][17] When King was 11, his family moved to Durham, Maine, where his mother cared for her parents until their deaths. She then became a caregiver in a local residential facility for the menty chenged.[1] King was raised Methodist,[18][19] but lost his belief in organized religion while in high school. While no longer religious, he says he chooses to believe in the existence of God.[20] As a child, King apparently witnessed one of his friends being struck and killed by a train, though he has no memory of the event. His family told him that after leaving to play with the boy, King returned speechless and seemingly in shock. later did the family learn of the death. Some commentators have suggested that this event may have psychologicy inspired some of King's darker works,[21] but King makes no mention of it in his memoir On Writing (2000). He related in detail his primary inspiration for writing horror fiction in his non-fiction Danse Macabre (1981), in a chapter titled "An Annoying Autobiographical Pause". He compared his uncle's dowsing for water using the bough of an apple branch with the sudden realization of what he wanted to do for a living. That inspiration occurred while browsing through an attic with his elder brother, when King uncovered a paperback version of an H. P. Lovecraft collection of short stories he remembers as The Lurker in the Shadows, that had belonged to his father. King told Barnes & Noble Studios in a 2009 interview, "I k that I'd found when I read that book."[22] King attended Durham Elementary School and graduated from Lisbon High School (Maine) in Lisbon Fs, Maine, in 1966.[23] He displayed an early interest in horror as an avid reader of EC horror comics, including Tales from the Crypt, and he later paid tribute to the comics in his screenplay for Creepshow. He began writing for fun while in school, contributing articles to Dave's Rag, the spaper his brother published with a mimeograph machine, and later began selling stories to his friends based on movies he had seen. (He was forced to return the when it was discovered by his teachers.) The first of his stories to be independently published was "I Was a Teenage Grave Robber", which was serialized over four issues (three published and one unpublished) of a fanzine, Comics Review, in 1965. It was republished the follog year in revised, as "In a Half-World of Terror", in another fanzine, Stories of Suspense, edited by Marv Wolfman.[24] As a tееn, King also a Scholastic Art and Writing Award.[25] King entered the University of Maine in 1966, and graduated in 1970 with a Bachelor of Arts in English.[26] That year, his daughter Naomi Rachel was born. He wrote a column, Steve King's Garbage Truck, for the student spaper, The Maine Campus, and participated in a writing workshop organized by Burton Hatlen.[27] King held a variety of jobs to pay for his studies, including as a janitor, a gas-station attendant, and an industrial laundry worker. He met his, fellow student Tabitha Spruce, at the university's Raymond H. Fogler Library after one of Professor Hatlen's workshops; they wed in 1971.[27] Career Beginnings In 1971, King worked as a teacher at Hampden Academy. King sold his first professional short story, "The Glass Floor", to Startling Mystery Stories in 1967.[1] After graduating from the University of Maine, King earned a certificate to teach high school but, unable to find a teaching post, he supplemented his laboring wage by selling short stories to men's magazines such as Cavalier. Many of these early stories were republished in the collection Night Shift. The short story "The Raft" was published in Adam, a men's magazine. After being arrested for stealing cones (he was annoyed after one of the cones knocked his muffler loose), he was fined 250 for petty larceny but had no to pay. However, a then arrived for "The Raft" (then titled "The Float"), and King cashed it to pay the fine.[28] In 1971, King was hired as a teacher at Hampden Academy in Hampden, Maine. He continued to contribute short stories to magazines and worked on ideas for novels.[1] During 1966–1970, he wrote a draft about his dystopian novel ced The Long Walk[29] and the anti-war novel Sword in the Darkness,[30][31] but neither of the works was published at the time; The Long Walk was later released in 1979. Carrie and aftermath In 1973, King's novel, Carrie, was accepted by publishing house, Doubleday. It was King's fourth novel,[32] but the first to be published. He wrote it on his Tabitha's portable typewriter. It began as a short story intended for Cavalier magazine, but King tossed the first three pages in the garbage can.[33] Tabitha recovered the pages and encouraged him to finish the story, saying she would help him with the female perspective; he followed her advice and expanded it into a novel.[34] He said: "I persisted because I was dry and had no better ideas... My considered opinion was that I had written the world's -time loser."[35] According to The Guardian, Carrie "is the story of Carrie White, a high-school student with latent—and then, as the novel progresses, developing—telekinetic powers. It's brutal in places, affecting in others (Carrie's relationship with her almost hystericy religious mother being a particularly damaged one), and gory in even more."[36] When Carrie was chosen for publication, King's was out of service. Doubleday editor William Thompson—who became King's close—sent a telegram to King's house in late March or early April 1973[37] which read: "Carrie Officiy A Doubleday Book. 2,500 Advance Against Royalties. Congrats, Kid – The Future Lies Ahead, Bill."[38] King said he bought a Ford Pinto with the advance.[37] On May 13, 1973, American Library bought the paperback rights for 400,000, which—in accordance with King's contract with Doubleday—was split between them.[39][40] Carrie set King's career in motion and became a significant novel in the horror genre. In 1976, it was made into a successful horror film.[41] King's 'Salem's Lot was published in 1975. In a 1987 issue of The Highway Patrolman magazine, he said, "The story seems sort of down home to me. I have a special cold spot in my heart for it!"[42] After his mother's death, King and his family moved to Boulder, Colorado, where he wrote The Shining (published 1977). The family returned to Auburn, Maine in 1975, where he completed The Stand (published 1978). In 1977, the family, with the addition of Owen Philip, his third and youngest child, traveled briefly to England. They returned to Maine that f, where King began teaching creative writing at the University of Maine.[43] In 1982, King published Different Seasons, a collection of four novellas with a more dramatic bent than the horror fiction for which he is famous.[44] It is notable for having three of its four novellas turned into Hollywood films: Stand by Me (1986) was adapted from The Body;[45] The Shawshank Redemption (1994) was adapted from Rita Hayworth and Shawshank Redemption;[46] and Apt Pupil (1998) was adapted from the novella of the same .[47][48] In 1985, King wrote his first work for the comic book,[49] writing a few pages of the benefit X-Men comic book Heroes for Hope Starring the X-Men. The book, whose were donated to famine relief in Africa, was written by a number of different authors in the comic book field, such as Chris Claremont, Stan Lee, and Alan Moore, as well as authors not primarily associated with comics, such as Harlan Ellison.[50] The follog year, King published It (1986), which was t-selling hardcover novel in the United States that year,[51] and wrote the introduction to Batman No. 400, an anniversary issue where he expressed his preference for the character over Superman.[52][53] The Dark Tower books Main article: The Dark Tower (series) In the late 1970s, King began what became a series of interconnected stories about a lone gunslinger, Roland, who pursues the "Man in Black" in an alternate-reality universe that is a cross between J. R. R. Tolkien's Middle-earth and the American Wild West as depicted by Clint Eastwood and Sergio Leone in their spaghetti Westerns. The first of these stories, The Dark Tower: The Gunslinger, was initiy published in five instments by The Magazine of Fantasy & Science Fiction under the editorship of Edward L. Ferman, from 1977 to 1981. The Gunslinger was continued as an eight-book epic series ced The Dark Tower, whose books King wrote and published infrequently over four decades (1978-2012).[54] Pseudonyms In the late 1970s and early 1980s, King published a handful of short novels—Rage (1977), The Long Walk (1979), Roadwork (1981), The Running Man (1982) and Thinner (1984)—under the pseudonym Richard Bachman. The idea behind this was to test whether he could replicate his again and to ay his fears that his popularity was an accident. An alternate explanation was that publishing standards at the time owed a single book a year.[55] He picked up the from the Canadian hard rock band Bachman–Turner Overdrive, of which he is a fan.[56] Richard Bachman was exposed as King's pseudonym by a persistent Washington, D.C. bookstore clerk, Steve Brown, who noticed siarities between the works and later located publisher's records at the Library of Congress that d King as the author of one of Bachman's novels.[57] This led to a press release heralding Bachman's "death"—supposedly from "cancer of the pseudonym".[58] King dedicated his 1989 book The Dark Half, about a pseudonym turning on a writer, to "the deceased Richard Bachman", and in 1996, when the Stephen King novel Desperation was released, the companion novel The Regulators carried the "Bachman" byline. In 2006, during a press conference in London, King declared that he had discovered another Bachman novel, titled Blaze. It was published on June 12, 2007. In fact, the original manuscript had been held at King's Alma mater, the University of Maine in Orono, for many years and had been covered by numerous King experts. King rewrote the original 1973 manuscript for its publication.[59] King has used other pseudonyms. The short story "The Fifth Quarter" was published under the pseudonym John Swithen (the of a character in the novel Carrie), by Cavalier in April 1972.[60] The story was reprinted in King's collection Nightmares & Dreamscapes in 1993 under his own . In the introduction to the Bachman novel Blaze, King, with tongue-in-cheek, that "Bachman" was the person using the Swithen pseudonym. The "children's book" Charlie the Choo-Choo: From the World of The Dark Tower was published in 2015 under the pseudonym Beryl Evans, who was portrayed by actress ison Davies during a book signing at San Diego Comic-Con,[61] and illustrated by Ned Dameron. It is adapted from a fictional book central to the plot of King's previous novel The Dark Tower III: The Waste Lands.[62] Digital era Stephen King at the Harvard Book Store, June 6, 2005 In 2000, King published online a serialized horror novel, The Plant.[63] At first the public assumed that King had abandoned the project because were unsuccessful, but King later stated that he had simply run out of stories.[64] The unfinished epistolary novel is still available from King's official site, . Also in 2000, he wrote a digital novella, Riding the Bullet, and saying he foresaw e-books becoming 50 of the market "probably by 2013 and maybe by 2012". If AR really does become “as big as smartphones,” the companies that own the IP and the worlds people actually spend time in could be the biggest winners. Elf Labs isn’t just experimenting with AR – they’re building the ecosystem Tim Cook was talking about. body:

------------------------------------------------------------
By Jon Fetherston on Dec 19, 2025 06:57 am
A once-quiet coastal town has been thrown into turmoil after local officials voted to allow an 8-year-old biological boy who identifies as a girl to compete on a recreational girls’ basketball team, triggering a parent revolt, police-monitored meetings, and the creation of a costly private alternative league. Dear Reader, It’s perhaps the most common coin in existence. I’m talking about the U.S. penny.
Recently, President Trump decided to kill the coin, for good reason. It now costs 4 cents to make a single penny. Which means the government is losing 3 cents on every one it mints.
---
[](https://links.firearmupgrades.com/a/1537/click/22262/6374453/3941149e4112a8e73b546132ade91b0c6fbe5653/b9a1d8c93bc9933e94ef57d9572c769937a137e7)
---
---
But the truth behind Trump’s decision may be stranger than you think. You see, the U.S. is facing a looming shortage that could cripple the economy with runaway inflation... and send one tiny clutch of investments soaring in the weeks ahead. Former White House Advisor, Jim Rickards, just came forward to share [**this startling story**](https://links.firearmupgrades.com/a/1537/click/22262/6374453/3941149e4112a8e73b546132ade91b0c6fbe5653/b9a1d8c93bc9933e94ef57d9572c769937a137e7). Along with the reason why millionaires and billionaires are moving a vast sum of money into a little-understood corner of the stock market. | | Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw
---
Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18]

This isn’t decades away. Foxconn plans to begin production in 2026 — right here in the U.S. — rolling out two advanced models that could replace millions of workers.

**What happens to your retirement when Washington shuts down again?**

Not “if.” **When.**

We just watched Congress nearly grind the entire system to a halt — and it’s only getting more chaotic.

And your retirement is tied to the same unstable system.

If your savings live inside government-dependent accounts, you’re one budget fight away from being stuck in the crossfire.

That’s why more Americans are building a [shutdown-proof backup plan](https://links.research.firstinvestmentupdates.com/u/click?_t=6c5e8d0087884bb7b7cd2e3abb0e6b5f&_m=e96e97a272a04fa39499d176edd16e6b&_e=_bk2Fc0d9GawTzMZfu0P4TGfuZLnOQR5KOYw6IiCu5rlaTbvEpQZoeDc9iN2ZGZe7cduiv2TEaZ4hYV5neNwHtGzoweMmVx5l50Ow3SV2soCBjgXeVhZUC4cixIXUZTfLhwnnZJ6Bay36kH3qFxxw3_stjuqYvTVx7jEsuAmS3VkRtj6O0t3d-B0lST6uzpj_nAR9ElZKwN23QxfHsb1roTHYeBOX6mBQt0ZjMDsoiN9sdH2WCXh1A4t2IGT38Dvwl-myPOQLgo-P7xaO3vgeyT_bICMojW3Y0PY-EvkPjWtPO8NP75nxGS1CyufoSzXV03mZcidRfn1fztVRSUuGiTF4d7zkmYaPhkyrny-bTxN6xr3H5WiV7d_Bv4ZazxQqyise8I8r43FpCYWb65ER-NbLPZqfBuj_RRI5wrU3P8rOYx2uHpnGQ8MFqOQratc8trYSXsjndtMi5OxX1tItacuD3cpbhWU6TIImlIXSuUJJZGqTS_3hZkEfNPAUMxjwWJCiHUAOtr6sekETrP9bpPTUpRlvkviTchGWTlpXt6yIf1zwZVXPZ9HrHi-9WYl1gJkGv-607qvLexJhLKr57E-HMtOWvSJ8-pNNtRn9mJ06aRx67F3uHn7eYlybKHf) with physical gold and silver.

Because metals don’t:

* Pause when Congress can’t agree

clean_body: What happens to your retirement when Washington shuts down again? Not “if.” When.| | | | | | "First Investment Update" | | | | | What happens to your retirement when Washington shuts down again? Not “if.” When. We just watched Congress nearly grind the entire system to a halt — and it’s only getting more chaotic. And your retirement is tied to the same unstable system. If your savings live inside government-dependent accounts, you’re one budget fight away from being stuck in the crossfire. That’s why more Americans are building a with physical gold and silver. Because metals don’t: Pause when Congress can’t agree Freeze because an agency closes * Lose value just because Washington stalled a vote The shows you exactly how to move part of your IRA/401(k) into tangible assets that stay yours — shutdown or no shutdown. If the government stops, your life shouldn’t. Reagan Gold Group does not provide financial, legal, or tax advice. This material is for educational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice. All investments carry risk, including loss of principal. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Please consult a licensed financial advisor before making investment decisions. Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18] | | | First Invеstmеnt Updаtеs brоught tо уоu bу Іnception Media, LLC.
