clean_body: Election News Update from the Grant Institute | | | | | | | | | | | | | | The Long Shadow of Bush v. Gore By Justin Riemer, President, USGI, and Marshal Trigg, Junior Counsel, USGI Twenty-five years ago this month, the Supreme Court decided Bush v. Gore , settling a presidential election determined by 537 votes out of nearly six million cast in Florida—a margin of 0.009%. The 2000 election was infamous for, among other things, “hanging chads,” but the broader lesson was about how the most technical flaws in election processes can suddenly become decisive in a close race. Palm Beach County’s butterfly ballot design confused many voters, and Duval County’s split-page ballot caused thousands of overvotes. These issues affected only a small percentage of votes, but in such a close election, everything mattered. The outcome of that election was highly divisive. Yet a resounding bipartisan consensus quickly emerged that we needed to fix persistent systemic weaknesses in election administration to avoid a repeat of 2000. Congress responded with the Help America Vote Act (HAVA) of 2002. HAVA upgraded and modernized voting systems by replacing punch-card machines and ensured systems allowed voters to verify their selections and detect overvotes. It also required states to offer provisional ballots. Since 2003, states have received approximately . Recognizing that accurate voter rolls are a cornerstone to effective election administration, HAVA also mandated statewide computerized registration lists. The National Voter Registration Act (NVRA) of 1993 had already established some national registration standards, requiring states to offer registration at DMVs and public assistance agencies and take certain steps to maintain clean voter rolls. It is hard to argue that the NVRA reflected the same truly bipartisan process as HAVA. President George H.W. Bush vetoed an earlier version of the bill and only a few Republicans for its final passage. Although the NVRA explicitly balance access and integrity, few conservatives would agree. It is not a wonder that the NVRA has left, for many, a 30-plus year bad aftertaste. Perhaps it shouldn’t be surprising then that a Republican Congress is looking at changes. The Committee on House Administration has held a series of hearings looking at how to modernize and improve the NVRA, especially its list maintenance provisions. Last week, its Subcommittee on Elections the issue following the full committee’s where USGI President Justin Riemer testified on the NVRA’s weaknesses. A few common threads emerged. One is the 90-day pre-election period prohibiting systematic voter removals, cited by both Florida State Law Professor Michael Morley and Riemer. Morley noted the NVRA’s ambiguity regarding what activities can take place during the 90-day blackout period. Does “remove” refer to the process of inactivating a voter or removing them from the voter rolls completely? He also urged Congress to clarify that the prohibition on “systematic” removals does not reach individualized determinations on ineligibility. Both witnesses stressed the blackout applies to primary and general elections, thus limiting systematic removals for half, or more, of each federal election year. Witnesses also highlighted how courts have inconsistently interpreted other ambiguous NVRA provisions, including that list maintenance be “uniform” and “nondiscriminatory” and that states make a “reasonable effort” to remove ineligible voters. The hearings also reflect how the NVRA is showing its age. It fails to specifically address removing or screening out noncitizen registrants (much less of a concern in 1993), electronic transactions, and heavily relies on using USPS mail processes for list maintenance. Expectedly, the Democratic minority witness last week emphasized how the NVRA helped increase voter registration rates and warned about aggressive list maintenance practices. And there was at least some overlap across the aisle on modernization: the Democratic witness, along with Republican witness Mark Braden, floated amending the NVRA to require states to offer online voter registration. Given the tight majorities in Congress and the intense ideological divide on voting issues, NVRA reforms may require unrealistic compromises. Democratic proposals for automatic or election day registration are likely nonstarters, at least without accompanying robust eligibility verification requirements. And progressives will undoubtedly bristle at Republican demands to enhance the NVRA’s list maintenance requirements. Still, the fact that Republicans are entertaining federal reforms is notable. For years, Republican conventional wisdom has maintained that reopening the NVRA framework carries more risk than upside. But persistent frustrations with the NVRA appear to be pushing some to reconsider. Beyond the partisan angling, states are increasingly pressed to answer the same practical questions: how should we verify eligibility and keep rolls accurate in an era of greater population mobility? And how can we better leverage technology, particularly data-sharing capabilities between government agencies? The world has changed. It took a rude wake up call for Congress to recognize this 25 years ago—maybe it will consider acting before another one. | | | Alabama supports legislation that would tighten disclosures for voter registration information and cap fees on voter list data. The proposal seeks to limit the sharing of sensitive personally identifiable information for commercial use as well. Alaska The mailed “voter registration confirmation notices” as part of its annual list maintenance process to verify whether registered voters still reside at their listed addresses. Officials say this outreach, required by state law and the National Voter Registration Act, helps ensure the voter rolls remain accurate ahead of future elections. District of Columbia The D.C. Council voted down a proposal to delay implementation of , clearing the way for the city’s first use of the system in the June 2026 mayoral and council primaries. The effort to postpone the rollout failed 8-5. Georgia The Secretary of State’s office said it is poll worker accused of scanning multiple ballots cast by a family member during a recent runoff election. Election officials reported the worker did not follow proper procedures by handling a relative’s ballot more than once, prompting review of the circumstances and possible charges. Illinois The said the office will not accept mobile or digital driver’s licenses as valid ID for voter services requiring ID. Instead, individuals must present a physical form of ID that meets state requirements. County officials said this interpretation reflects their assessment of current law and guidance. Indiana The state voted down a proposed redistricting plan that would have redrawn legislative districts ahead of the 2026 elections. The defeat came after Republican leaders failed to secure enough support from within their own caucus. Opponents of the proposal argued the map favored incumbents and lacked sufficient bipartisan input, forcing lawmakers back to the drawing board. Kansas resigned as mayor of Coldwater after being charged with illegally voting in multiple elections and related offenses due to his noncitizen status. Prosecutors say the case followed an investigation into his voting history, and charges include voting without being qualified and election perjury. The situation drew nationwide attention as a rare criminal allegation tied to a local elected official’s conduct. Maryland Republicans from the Committee on House Administration sent a letter demanding answers from the State Board of Elections after voter registration records showed Ian Andre Roberts, an illegal alien and former Iowa school superintendent was listed as a registered voter in Prince George’s County. Previously, the county had released the unredacted voter records for Roberts, following a legal threat by Restoring Integrity and Elections (RITE) and the American Accountability Foundation (AAF). Minnesota So far 113,047 Minnesotans have opted into a that lets voters join a permanent absentee ballot list, meaning they automatically receive a mail ballot for every election without reapplying. Mississippi A woman was convicted under a ballot harvesting law for collecting and transmitting an absentee ballot for another person and received a six-month suspended sentence plus a $2,718 fine. She says she did nothing wrong helping senior citizens with their ballots and has filed an appeal arguing with how the statute was applied. The case stems from actions during the 2023 Democratic primaries under a law that bars non-family members from submitting ballots on others’ behalf. Ohio The state joined a called EleXa with nine other states to compare voter registration information to help identify voters with registrations in multiple states and improve voter-roll accuracy. Secretary of State LaRose said the agreements are intended to catch illegal out-of-state voting and maintain accurate rolls after Ohio left the Electronic Registration Information Center (ERIC) in 2023. The Secretary of State’s office launched the to provide ongoing training and credentialing for election officials across the state. The program requires newly appointed election board members to complete specific training credits within their first year and requires continuing education annually for incumbents. It centralizes instructional content and resources to help ensure officials stay current on laws and administration practices. Pennsylvania say problems with third-party voter registration drives, including large batches of incomplete or falsified forms, create administrative burdens and can overwhelm local offices ahead of deadlines. Following the Attorney General charging six individuals with falsifying registration forms, officials want changes to how registration organizations operate to reduce invalid submissions and ease processing work for elections staff. Tennessee The Dickson County Election Commission a new elections administrator at a recent meeting, but two of the five commissioners raised concerns about how the selection process was conducted. Texas Following the voter list technology upgrade at the Secretary of State’s office, their candidate portal was part of another technology update, also . This created issues with counties trying to find candidates who were qualifying for the upcoming primary. Virginia A state lawmaker is to put Virginia back into ERIC following the state’s withdrawal in May 2023. Wisconsin The voted 5 to 1 to decline a Department of Justice demand for the state’s unredacted voter registration file, saying state law prohibits sharing sensitive information such as partial Social Security numbers, driver’s license numbers, and dates of birth. The letter to DOJ noted the commission can only provide publicly available, redacted voter information, and said the request exceeds what state law allows. On December 4, the ACLU of Delaware in Delaware Superior Court challenging the Town of Fenwick Island’s practice of allowing artificial entities such as corporations to register and cast votes in municipal elections. Maine United States v. Bellows, No. 1:25-cv-1379 (D. Me.) DeMarinis, No. 1:25-cv-3989 (D. Md.) The lawsuit states that at least two of Maryland’s largest counties report voter-registration totals exceeding the number of adult citizens aged 18 and over. Michigan Allen v. City of Hamtramck, No. 25-_-CK (Wayne Cnty. Cr. Ct.) A Hamtramck city clerk in state court against the City of Hamtramck and various city officials, alleging retaliation and related claims after she was placed on administrative leave in the aftermath of the contested November 4 mayoral election. The plaintiff contends she was suspended in retaliation for reporting alleged election misconduct and fraud to the Michigan Attorney General’s office earlier in the year. Missouri People Not Politicians v. Hoskins, No. 25AC-CC07128 (Mo. Cir. Ct. Cole Cnty.) On December 12, a state court over whether Missouri Secretary of State Hoskins can decline to count thousands of signatures collected on a referendum petition aimed at overturning the state’s newly enacted congressional redistricting map. The suit challenges Hoskins’ position that signatures gathered before October 14 should not count toward qualifying the referendum for the November 2026 ballot. Montana Chippewa Cree Indians of the Rocky Boy’s Rsrv. v. Chouteau Cnty., No. 4:25-cv-00069-BMM (D. Mont.) On December 15, 2025, a federal court that requires Chouteau County to redraw its commission districts to create a single-member district encompassing the Rocky Boy’s Reservation. Plaintiffs had argued that the at-large voting system diluted the voting strength of tribal voters. Nevada United States v. Aguilar, No. 3:25-cv-00728 (D. Nev.) On December 16, the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) and its Nevada chapter in the DOJ’s federal lawsuit against Nevada election officials over access to the state’s detailed voter registration data. New Hampshire Robertson v. Scanlan, No. 218-2025-CV-000951 (N.H. Super. Ct. Rockingham Cnty.) On December 12, a state court a challenge brought by three voters against New Hampshire’s new absentee ballot identification requirements. The plaintiffs argued the statute imposes undue burdens and discriminates against voters with disabilities. The court ruled that the law imposes reasonable, nondiscriminatory obligations and does not unconstitutionally burden the right to vote. New Jersey Barberio v. Desai, No. L.-002675-25 (N.J. Super. Ct. Morris Cnty.) On December 5, the incumbent mayor of Parsippany in Morris County Superior Court contesting the results of the town’s November 4, 2025 mayoral election after Democrat Pulkit Desai was certified the winner by just 80 votes. Barberio’s complaint alleges discrepancies and irregularities in the vote-by-mail count, including a purported mismatch between the number of mail-in ballots tallied and those officially recorded as received by the deadline, as well as counting errors by the Morris County Board of Canvassers. In re Tom Malinowski, Nos. A-3542-21, A-3543-21 (N.J. Super. Ct. App. Div.) The state Supreme Court to the state’s longstanding ban on fusion voting, leaving intact lower-court rulings upholding the prohibition. The case sought to invalidate New Jersey election laws that prevent multiple political parties from nominating the same candidate and having that candidate appear on the ballot under more than one party line. Ohio NRSC v. FEC, No. 1:22-cv-00639 (S.D. Ohio) On December 9, the Supreme Court in a major constitutional challenge to federal campaign-finance limits on coordinated party expenditures. A decision is expected by late June or early July. Pennsylvania United States v. Laiss, No. 25-386 (E.D. Pa.) On December 8, a federal court on whether a broad presidential pardon issued by President Donald Trump should be construed to cover the federal double-voting and voter-fraud charges against defendant Matthew Laiss. Laiss is accused of voting twice in the 2020 general election — once by mail in Bucks County, Pennsylvania, and once in person in Florida — allegedly casting both votes for Trump. Rhode Island United States v. Amore, No. 1:25-cv-00639 (D.R.I.) On December 9, Common Cause Rhode Island as a defendant in the DOJ’s lawsuit seeking access to Rhode Island’s statewide voter-registration database and related election records. South Carolina NAACP S.C. State Conference v. Wilson, No. 3:25-cv-13754 (D.S.C.) On December 5, the NAACP and three voters challenging state voting assistance restrictions that plaintiffs say unlawfully burden voters with disabilities. The plaintiffs allege that existing state law severely limits who may assist voters with disabilities in requesting or returning absentee ballots and imposes felony penalties on those who provide help outside narrow categories. Wisconsin United States v. Town of Thornapple, No. 3:24-CV-00664 (W.D. Wis.) On December 12, the town of Thornapple with DOJ to provide at least one voting system accessible to voters with disabilities at each polling place, resolving DOJ lawsuit that followed the town’s 2024 decision to prohibit electronic voting machines in federal elections. | | | | The U.S. House Committee on Administration’s Elections Subcommittee held a hearing on December 10 “Examining Potential Updates to the NVRA.” A video of the proceedings and witness statements are available . The American Enterprise Institute recently sponsored a daylong conference marking the 25th Anniversary of the Bush v. Gore decision. A replay of the conference is available . AEI’s John Fortier and EAC Commissioner Don Palmer also hosted Michael Carvin, a key player in the litigation, on their . An states that the expanded federal Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements (SAVE) database, now used by some jurisdictions to check citizenship, has incorrectly flagged U.S. citizens as non-citizens and possibly led to wrongful removal from voter lists and added documentation requests. Critics argue SAVE was not designed for voter eligibility verification and that errors could disenfranchise eligible voters if states rely on its results without additional safeguards. | | | looks at how the U.S. Supreme Court allowed Texas’s mid-decade congressional map to stand for the 2026 midterms and how the ruling could discourage federal courts from blocking states’ late changes to election laws or maps. The opinion emphasized that lower federal courts should not intervene too close to an election, potentially giving state legislatures greater latitude to enact or revise election rules well before ballots are cast. Observers worry this interpretation of the “Purcell” principle could make it harder to challenge controversial election changes in federal court ahead of 2026. | | | paper provides an overview of how artificial intelligence is reshaping election administration, voter information, and campaign environments. It identifies key risks, including AI-generated misinformation, synthetic candidates or voter contacts, and vulnerabilities in AI-assisted administrative tools, and evaluates their implications for election officials. The authors outline policy options for states and federal agencies to strengthen preparedness, improve transparency, and mitigate emerging AI-related threats to election integrity. The paper explains a DHS proposal to incorporate state motor-vehicle records into the federal Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements (SAVE) system, which election officials increasingly use to check voter citizenship. It outlines how SAVE works, discusses privacy and accuracy risks tied to driver’s-license data, and stresses that new data sources might increase inconclusive or incorrect matches that election offices must resolve. Computer Science Professor finds that “VoteSecure,” a proposal to enable voting by smartphone, suffers from fundamental vulnerabilities that make secure and verifiable mobile voting unattainable with current technology. The author explains that end-to-end verification methods still risk exposing voter preferences, threatening ballot secrecy or failing to prevent malware and server manipulation. The piece underscores that all known internet-voting protocols, including the one advocated by the Mobile Voting Foundation, have gaps and impracticalities that render them too insecure for public elections. | | | The Grant Institute has potential openings for junior level attorneys and others interested in the study of election administration and voting issues. Interested candidates should please email their resume to mailto:info@grantdemocracy.org. Disney is hiring a . Finally, please note that the Grant Report will be taking a holiday hiatus and resume on January 9, 2026. We wish all our readers a restful and joyous holiday season. | [Please reply to this email or email info@grantdemocracy.org to sign up for the Grant Report and other USGI updates.]() | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | Copyright (C) 2025 The Ulysses S. Grant Institute for the Study of Democracy. All rights reserved._

name: Wes Moore for Maryland
subject: Focused
body:
Fighting for results.
# [](https://act.wesmoore.com/go/8?t=1&akid=1088%2E1086043%2Edbcetl)
Peter, we have to use every single minute of every single day to fight for results for the people who make up this grassroots movement.
**Wes knows it:** It’s why he keeps a countdown clock on his desk that counts down the days left in his term.
**It’s how he’s been able to get so much done:** historic progress on public safety, record investments in our schools, and standing up for everyone from federal workers to small businesses.
We have to stay focused and bring that same energy to reelection, Peter. **[If you’re with us in that fight, chip in now.](https://act.wesmoore.com/go/417?c0=wes-em-20251219-1.ObblEl&refcode=em-20251219-1&t=2&akid=1088%2E1086043%2Edbcetl)**
We only have days until the end-of-year fundraising deadline, and we need your support to reach our goals.
Like Wes says, “Things don’t happen. Things are made to happen.”
**[Reaching our fundraising goal won’t happen by accident — we have to make it there together. We’ve made too much progress to stop now. Can Wes count on you to chip in $5 or any amount today?](https://act.wesmoore.com/go/417?c0=wes-em-20251219-1.ObblEl&refcode=em-20251219-1&t=3&akid=1088%2E1086043%2Edbcetl)**
**[Donate $5 ››](https://act.wesmoore.com/go/417?c0=wes-em-20251219-1.ObblEl&amount=5&refcode=em-20251219-1&t=22&akid=1088%2E1086043%2Edbcetl)**
Let’s make sure we don’t reset the clock on our progress.
Together,
Team Wes Moore for Maryland

* * *
**[Donate »](https://secure.actblue.com/donate/wes-em-footer?refcode=em-20251219-1&t=23&refcode2=1088_1086043_dbcetl&akid=1088%2E1086043%2Edbcetl)**
This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com. Email is a critical way we communicate with grassroots supporters like you. However, if you would like to change your subscription preferences, here are some options:
* [Receive Fewer Emails](https://act.wesmoore.com/signup/receive-fewer-emails/?t=24&akid=1088%2E1086043%2Edbcetl)
* [Update Your Information](https://act.wesmoore.com/signup/update-your-information/)
* [Unsubscribe](https://act.wesmoore.com/unsubscribe/unsubscribe/?t=25&akid=1088%2E1086043%2Edbcetl)

Email us: [info@wesmoore.com](mailto:info@wesmoore.com)
BY AUTHORITY: WES MOORE FOR MARYLAND, MARY TYDINGS, TREASURER

name: Haley S.
subject: This is personal
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Haley Stevens for Senate
body:
I’m not backing down

This email was sent to dpwillis67@gmail.com. [To unsubscribe from this email list, please click here.](https://act.haleyformi.com/unsubscribe/unsubscribe/?&t=1&akid=1677%2E49239%2E7l6--f)
| | | [](https://act.haleyformi.com/go/10605?t=2&akid=1677%2E49239%2E7l6--f)
---
null, it’s Haley.

I want to talk to you for a moment, not as a politician running for office, but as a woman from Michigan.
You may have seen the news: **An anti-abortion group announced they’re going to spend $80 million to help Republicans win the Senate. And they’ve named Michigan a top target.**
They’re launching ads and door-to-door campaigns to support MAGA extremists who back a near-total abortion ban – like my opponent Mike Rogers.

Let me be clear: I’ve spent my career fighting for reproductive freedom. I’ve stood with Planned Parenthood, I’ve defended access to care, and I’ve worked to restore the rights we had under Roe.
And I’m not going to stop now.

This is personal.

**_[If you’re with me, will you rush $5 to help me fight back and keep Michigan blue?](https://act.haleyformi.com/go/10606?t=3&akid=1677%2E49239%2E7l6--f)_**
| | [Donate](https://act.haleyformi.com/go/10607?t=4&akid=1677%2E49239%2E7l6--f)
---
Here’s the bottom line: This race could decide control of the Senate. It could decide whether abortion rights survive in our country.

Mike Rogers has Trump’s endorsement and the backing of a MAGA Texas billionaire.
But I’ve got you.

So if you believe in reproductive freedom, I’m urgently asking you to **stand with me.[Rush $5 now to help us win this race and keep Michigan blue. ](https://act.haleyformi.com/go/10606?t=5&akid=1677%2E49239%2E7l6--f)**
With grit,

Haley
|
* * *
**A note from Haley Stevens:** null, I’m proud to be a lifelong Michigander and the daughter of small-business owners. That’s why I’ve fought so hard for working families my entire life. Now, I’m running for Michigan’s open Senate seat to continue fighting for the people and communities I love. The path to the Senate majority runs right through Michigan, which means this race is shaping up to be the most competitive and expensive in the entire country. The GOP is already coming after me as a top target for defeat, so I’m counting on your grassroots support to fight back and win. [**If you’re in this fight with me, will you add your name to endorse my campaign?**](https://act.haleyformi.com/go/10608?email=dpwillis67@gmail.com&zip=18326&t=6&akid=1677%2E49239%2E7l6--f) | | | [](https://act.haleyformi.com/go/10605?t=7&akid=1677%2E49239%2E7l6--f)
---
You can also text “JOIN” to 80242 to become a core member of our team and receive exclusive, real-time updates from my campaign.
Thank you,
Haley

[To unsubscribe from this email list, please click here.](https://act.haleyformi.com/unsubscribe/unsubscribe/?&t=8&akid=1677%2E49239%2E7l6--f)

name: Chris MacKenzie
subject: Save the Date: Strategic Impacts of AI-Enabled Cyber — January 21
body:
Join Helen Toner and Lt Gen (Ret) Jack Shanahan for an in-person discussion on AI-enabled cyber and U.S.-China dynamics. January 21, 2026.

| [ ](https://responsibleinnovation.us21.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a3c0dd7c48fa7fb81cbc8185a&id=efa916a335&e=a07d75591f)
---
| **Register for the live panel on January 21, 2-4 PM ET** | [Register Now](https://responsibleinnovation.us21.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a3c0dd7c48fa7fb81cbc8185a&id=14ef4c3d6c&e=a07d75591f)
---|---
AI is reshaping the cyber domain, with significant implications for strategic stability between the United States and China. Americans for Responsible Innovation is bringing together leading voices in AI and national security to examine what this means for policymakers and the future of deterrence.
| [Register for the Live Panel](https://responsibleinnovation.us21.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a3c0dd7c48fa7fb81cbc8185a&id=94f90b5811&e=a07d75591f)
---
| Confirmed Speakers | |
---
Helen Toner Senior Fellow, Georgetown's Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET)
|
---
Lt Gen (Ret) Jack Shanahan Adjunct Senior Fellow, CNAS; Founding Director, DoD Joint Artificial Intelligence Center
Additional panelists to be announced.
|
---
Moderator | | Morgan Plummer VP of Policy Design & Delivery, Americans for Responsible Innovation Former Senior Defense Official, OSD; Former Managing Director, National Security Innovation Network
---|---
The Discussion Will Address | → How AI-enabled cyber capabilities may compress decision-making timelines and limit human oversight
---
→ The implications of AI-obscured attribution on deterrence and crisis stability
→ Escalation pathways from cyber operations to broader conflict
→ Prospects for bilateral norms and codes of restraint
The panel will feature moderated discussion followed by audience Q&A.; Light refreshments will be provided.
|
---
This is an in-person event with limited capacity. Formal invitations with registration details will follow in early January. This is a widely attended event per House and Senate guidelines.* | [Register Now](https://responsibleinnovation.us21.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a3c0dd7c48fa7fb81cbc8185a&id=a959bf1f5d&e=a07d75591f)
---
Questions? Contact [info@responsibleinnovation.org](mailto:info@responsibleinnovation.org)
| Americans for Responsible Innovation Advancing AI policy that serves the public interest.
---

name: Mark Kelly
subject: My decision --
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Gillibrand for Senate
body:
We’ve been in some tough fights before, but this one is different. The President of the United States threatened my life, personally. I’ve got the entire executive branch bearing down on me right now.
[](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/121592402/589954321/1545041525?amount=10&source=LOC_EM_FR_2025.12.19_Mark-Kelly-Split_B1_F1_S1_C1_90AC-NC_RS-T0&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTkvMS83MjcyMiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIxMjAyYjYwNi1lYWRjLWYwMTEtODE5NC0wMDBkM2ExZWM4YzYiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=EIeYiKjhcIkqZs6I8NQ-JcRsFahCHlXF-AC_L5yhdxQ=) Hey folks, it’s Mark Kelly. Seven years ago, I was making a decision about whether I was going to run for the Senate. I knew it would be a lot of work. A lot of time away from home. A lot of time away from family. I knew it wouldn’t be easy. But after two years of Donald Trump and a Republican-held Senate doing just about everything he wanted, I knew I couldn’t sit on the sidelines. I had to do something. So, I ran. And, somehow, we won. And then we had to turn around and run all over again to win a full term after that. But thanks to your support, we won that one, too. I’m grateful every day that I get to wake up, walk out the door, and defend our people, our democracy, and our Constitution. It’s one of the greatest honors of my life. And I’ve never forgotten that I wouldn’t be here without your help. We’ve been in some tough fights before, but this one is different. The President of the United States threatened my life, personally. I’ve got the entire executive branch bearing down on me right now. After everything you’ve already done for me, I shouldn’t have to ask you for more. But that’s what I need to do. **[I’m asking you to split a donation between Kirsten Gillibrand and me, and I hope you know that I wouldn’t be asking if this wasn’t important.](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/121592403/589954322/1545041525?amount=10&source=LOC_EM_FR_2025.12.19_Mark-Kelly-Split_B1_F1_S1_C1_90AC-NC_RS-T0&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTkvMS83MjcyMiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIxMjAyYjYwNi1lYWRjLWYwMTEtODE5NC0wMDBkM2ExZWM4YzYiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=EIeYiKjhcIkqZs6I8NQ-JcRsFahCHlXF-AC_L5yhdxQ=)** _If you've saved payment information with ActBlue Express, your split donation to Mark Kelly and Kirsten Gillibrand will go through immediately:_ | | [DONATE $10 NOW >> ](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/121592404/589954323/1545041525?amount=10&source=LOC_EM_FR_2025.12.19_Mark-Kelly-Split_B1_F1_S1_C1_90AC-NC_RS-T0&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTkvMS83MjcyMiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICIxMjAyYjYwNi1lYWRjLWYwMTEtODE5NC0wMDBkM2ExZWM4YzYiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=EIeYiKjhcIkqZs6I8NQ-JcRsFahCHlXF-AC_L5yhdxQ=)

clean_body: We’ve been in some tough fights before, but this one is different. The President of the United States threatened my life, personally. I’ve got the entire executive branch bearing down on me right now. = Hey folks, it’s Mark Kelly. Seven years ago, I was making a decision about whether I was going to run for the Senate. I knew it would be a lot of work. A lot of time away from home. A lot of time away from family. I knew it wouldn’t be easy. But after two years of Donald Trump and a Republican-held Senate doing just about everything he wanted, I knew I couldn’t sit on the sidelines. I had to do something. So, I ran. And, somehow, we won. And then we had to turn around and run all over again to win a full term after that. But thanks to your support, we won that one, too. I’m grateful every day that I get to wake up, walk out the door, and defend our people, our democracy, and our Constitution. It’s one of the greatest honors of my life. And I’ve never forgotten that I wouldn’t be here without your help. We’ve been in some tough fights before, but this one is different. The President of the United States threatened my life, personally. I’ve got the entire executive branch bearing down on me right now. After everything you’ve already done for me, I shouldn’t have to ask you for more. But that’s what I need to do. = If you've saved payment information with ActBlue Express, your split donation to Mark Kelly and Kirsten Gillibrand will go through immediately: | | = | = | = | = | = Thank you. | | Dear Fellow American,

The national debt is currently over 37 trillion dollars.

If we don't get our national debt under control, it could be disastrous for America.

As a conservative -- and as a concerned American -- I encourage you to complete **The Heritage Foundation 's** **Taxpayer Ballot on Critical Spending Cuts.**

This ballot will gauge your views on government spending, and ridiculous funding priorities like DEI indoctrination.

Your responses will be used as a "wake-up call" to policymakers in Washington: That Americans voted for change in 2024 and want to see the spending cuts promised.

To make your voice heard on these critical issues, please complete our **Taxpayer Ballot on Critical Spending Cuts at the secure link below.**

name: Jose Javier Rodriguez
subject: The president says affordability is a scam
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY JOSE JAVIER RODRIGUEZ FOR FLORIDA ATTORNEY GENERAL
body:
Tell that to the 12 million Floridians crushed by FPL's rate hike. | [ ](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/121594185/589960386/1546157161?refcode=em_JJR_Affordability_121925&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNTkvMS8xMDkxMjYiLA0KICAiRGlzdHJpYnV0aW9uVW5pcXVlSWQiOiAiMWQ1NmQxNmItZWJkYy1mMDExLTgxOTQtMDAwZDNhMWVjOGM2IiwNCiAgIkVtYWlsQWRkcmVzcyI6ICJkcHdpbGxpczY3QGdtYWlsLmNvbSINCn0%3D&hmac=2uwzz96g86cTYaxS3rL2Bfc_lkaiJdPiFKXw9FwaR4c=&emci=2744c223-85db-f011-8195-000d3a1d58aa&emdi=1d56d16b-ebdc-f011-8194-000d3a1ec8c6&ceid=32374386)
---
Peter — Donald Trump says affordability is a “Democrat scam” and a “con job.” Tell that to the 12 million Floridians who just got hit with the largest utility rate hike in U.S. history. Tell that to families paying some of the highest insurance rates in the country. Or tell that to seniors deciding whether they can afford groceries or their electric bill. You’ll never hear a word of pushback from Florida’s leaders. They’re too busy trying to score political points instead of dealing with what people are actually going through. **Our MAGA Attorney General is no different. He cares more about impressing Trump and DeSantis than standing up for Floridians.** This is exactly why I’m running to defeat him. I want to bring transparency and accountability back to the Attorney General’s office. As the People’s Lawyer, I’ll take on the corporations and politicians driving up your costs, and I won’t hesitate to take them to court when they break the rules. **[Florida families deserve better. If you can, will you chip in $15 to power this campaign forward and help me become an Attorney General who will actually fight to lower costs?](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/121594189/589960396/-1819093735?amount=15&refcode=em_JJR_Affordability_121925&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNTkvMS8xMDkxMjYiLA0KICAiRGlzdHJpYnV0aW9uVW5pcXVlSWQiOiAiMWQ1NmQxNmItZWJkYy1mMDExLTgxOTQtMDAwZDNhMWVjOGM2IiwNCiAgIkVtYWlsQWRkcmVzcyI6ICJkcHdpbGxpczY3QGdtYWlsLmNvbSINCn0%3D&hmac=2uwzz96g86cTYaxS3rL2Bfc_lkaiJdPiFKXw9FwaR4c=)** _If you've saved your payment information with ActBlue Express, your donation will go through immediately:_ | **[DONATE $10](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/121594196/589960489/-1819093735?amount=10&express_lane=true&refcode=em_JJR_Affordability_121925&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQNTkvMS8xMDkxMjYiLA0KICAiRGlzdHJpYnV0aW9uVW5pcXVlSWQiOiAiMWQ1NmQxNmItZWJkYy1mMDExLTgxOTQtMDAwZDNhMWVjOGM2IiwNCiAgIkVtYWlsQWRkcmVzcyI6ICJkcHdpbGxpczY3QGdtYWlsLmNvbSINCn0%3D&hmac=2uwzz96g86cTYaxS3rL2Bfc_lkaiJdPiFKXw9FwaR4c=) **

Tell that to families paying some of the highest insurance rates in the country. Or tell that to seniors deciding whether they can afford groceries or their electric bill. You’ll never hear a word of pushback from Florida’s leaders. They’re too busy trying to score political points instead of dealing with what people are actually going through. Our MAGA Attorney General is no different. He cares more about impressing Trump and DeSantis than standing up for Floridians. This is exactly why I’m running to defeat him. I want to bring transparency and accountability back to the Attorney General’s office. name: Patrick J. Murphy
subject: From the Front with Patrick J. Murphy: Leadership, Service, and Building a Stronger Future
body:
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP25/1/106220/images/PJM_Logo.png]
From the Front with Patrick J. Murphy: Leadership, Service, and a Nation in Transition
Hi Friends,
It has been another meaningful week of work centered on moral leadership, accountability, and strengthening the communities we serve. As the holidays approach, it’s hard not to notice how many of these same themes are seen across the country. Wherever I go, I see people committed to the values of discipline, integrity, and service. It’s these values that will make us stronger in 2026.
In the Media: Conversations on Leadership and Policy
This week, I had the opportunity to appear on two media panels discussing the issues shaping Pennsylvania and the nation.
On December 16th, I joined Cassie Smedile and Harris Faulkner on Fox News on the evolving political landscape. We tackled two critical topics: the Democratic Party's internal challenges as progressives and establishment figures compete in primary races across the country, and the broader question of what voters are truly seeking (authenticity, economic solutions, and leaders willing to fight for working families).
The conversation highlighted how both parties are grappling with older party leadership, the transitions occurring and what that means for 2026 and beyond.
On December 17th, I taped an interview for NBC10 Today, which will air on Sunday, December 28th, at 10 am. The discussion centered on Governor Josh Shapiro's leadership in Pennsylvania, particularly focusing on recent crime data showing significant progress across the state.
The numbers tell an encouraging story: Philadelphia has seen homicides drop 17-18% by mid-year, SEPTA reported a 10% reduction in serious crimes, and violent offenses are decreasing in multiple areas. This improvement stems from our men and women in blue, data-driven policing, community-based violence interruption programs, and targeted enforcement focusing on repeat offenders and crime hotspots.
We also discussed Pennsylvania's economic future, including $31 billion in private investment, 16K more Pennsylvania jobs (while the nation lost 1.17 jobs this past year) and the massive tech investments in data centers that are creating high-paying construction and operations jobs while positioning the state at the center of America's AI infrastructure boom.
In the Media: How Economic Headwinds are Hurting Working Americans
On Wednesday, I joined Fox News’ America Reports to discuss rising grocery and utility costs, ongoing layoffs, and how economic uncertainty is affecting families across the country. We emphasized that Americans deserve leaders who can come together, strengthen our economy, and provide relief at home.
Today I’m back in NY to speak at the Fordham Business School and at 3pm I return to Fox News for a panel discussion on the global challenges shaping America’s security landscape. We’ll talk about what this moment demands responsible leaders and our need to navigate economic and geopolitical pressures while protecting our national interests and supporting our allies.
Across both conversations, our message remains focused on unity, strength, accountability and keeping America competitive and secure.
On Substack: What Veterans and Athletes Know About Leadership
This week on Substack, I wrote about our fourth annual Army–Navy Conference, held at Johns Hopkins Carey Business School, which has become a gathering place for athletes, veterans, executives, and public servants who share a common warrior ethos. I wrote about how discipline, accountability, and belonging continue to shape people long after their uniform comes off.
Through the stories of Maryland Governor Wes Moore, Under Armour founder & CEO Kevin Plank, and former NFL players Justin Forsett and Nate Boyer, I look at the shared values that carried them to the highest levels of their fields.
Read the full pieces here: What Veterans and Athletes Know About Leadership [https://open.substack.com/pub/patrickmurphypa/p/what-veterans-and-athletes-know-about?utm_campaign=post&utm_medium=web]
In Newsweek:
My first op-ed in Newsweek making the case that if America wants stronger companies, it needs more veterans in charge. That means hiring our vets as CEOs to run companies.
With a massive wave of 40% the 36 million small-business owners retiring, we’re staring at a generational opportunity: veterans and athletes stepping into leadership and ownership roles that keep America’s economy running.
Read the full piece here: Why Corporate America Needs More Veteran CEOs [https://open.substack.com/pub/patrickmurphypa/p/why-corporate-america-needs-more?utm_source=direct&utm_campaign=post-expanded-share&utm_medium=post%20viewer]
On Warrior Money: Army Navy Week - Why America misunderstands veteran disabilities
On this week’s episode of Warrior Money, we tackle one of the most persistent and damaging misconceptions about America’s veterans: the “broken veteran” narrative - which is constraint to the page try of tomorrow’s Army Navy Game. With millions of veterans using the VA healthcare system, we address why disability benefits 99% of the time aren’t fraud or weakness—they’re part of the contract the nation makes with those it sends into harm’s way.
Watch the full episode here: Warrior Money [https://youtu.be/O5xIu6RR56o?si=3V6us7WtrkLU6Cxg] .
Looking Back: The Army-Navy Conference in Baltimore
[https://prod.cdn.everyaction.com/images/van/NGP/NGP25/1/106220/images/IMG_5204.jpeg]
Last week, we hosted our fourth annual Warrior Class Army-Navy Conference at Johns Hopkins Carey Business School in Baltimore. We brought together veterans and business leaders who are bringing the lessons of service into boardrooms, statehouses, startups, and the agencies that keep our nation strong.
This year's speakers included Governor Wes Moore, Representative Chris Deluzio, Under Armour CEO Kevin Plank, ID.me CEO Blake Hall, Moonshots Capital Founder Kelly Perdew, Carlyle Group's Frank Finelli, Hilco Global Vice Chairman David Kurtz and their Chamber of Commerce’s Liz O’Brien.
The day was a gathering of veteran excellence (proof that service doesn't end when we hang up the uniform). It continues in the work we do every day to lift one another and strengthen this country.
Looking Ahead: Gratitude and Continued Service
As we move into the final weeks of 2025, I am grateful for the communities we've built and the conversations we've had. Veterans, families, and civic leaders are stepping up every day to make this country stronger.
Thank you for being part of this mission.
Hon. Patrick J. Murphy
32nd Under Secretary of the Army
P.S. Follow @patrickmurphypa on social media and subscribe to the weekly Substack [https://substack.com/@patrickmurphypa] for more updates on leadership, service, and innovation.

If you believe you received this message in error or wish to no longer receive email from us, please unsubscribe: https://secure.ngpvan.com/p/JcTKjLm2l0SduvIvv8SeUg2?unsubscribedata=zoAzvQ3jWKV0o7u%2FrtfRWYM09DuEXhq%2F%2F7UVA666mmP%2FTKGiynAE50OLPrFQsZON4ZS%2FbUUkL5YDDvqHl1WeCuxZ8KwHdDRz6Wv2QuEkaHZKgIwDzUVRmEVsTW61r0tRQgZfxrhu4js2chNxHhzxOb7JzEB5jJHqcX6gNwNx5%2FMeQH6sFWqP7EgpP5pYupbkgqwwrIR2YTgckuJSFdYyySY4w9TF%2BjNZJKJCeYjaQfWee4BTXmBAcDqZj3OjOp%2B%2FC2GRehJKm1%2F%2FkdEA%2BhGTfY%2FE%2BvZPubCv7l3%2BKzIFmaEwaeireQ2frgbUHOXcCkkhJUtnJEyq3a0DGeUQucfoF%2BpTHQkwWII0VNkeh4ua9mFG6dhOK9N4JIg4Mm3yUIy4tT9lSSWhr2a70FZGzpcxSGNAoDmAcJMTrMzXS68Aw1MpBXIVeljIC4o7HohlQ6EB .

name: Colleen Davis
subject: This is the worst state for women
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Her Bold Move Action
body:
Plus, a man who says, “Gender is god-given, and there’s only two of ‘em”... has no place in government.| | |
---
| | [](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/121594636/589961328/-1133336361?refcode=EM_F_251217worst_N_CD_90DO_13_G_N_Logo&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9FQS9FQTAwNy8xLzg4NDk1IiwNCiAgIkRpc3RyaWJ1dGlvblVuaXF1ZUlkIjogIjQ4MGVkNjcxLWViZGMtZjAxMS04MTk0LTAwMGQzYTFlYzhjNiIsDQogICJFbWFpbEFkZHJlc3MiOiAiZHB3aWxsaXM2N0BnbWFpbC5jb20iDQp9&hmac=N6bMKYjSMlvgw4wwfs0BfxLhR522xlKt7URkuPNrs08=)
---
Hi Peter,

As we near the end of 2025, national studies continue to rank Oklahoma as one of the worst states (perhaps THE worst state) for women.

Oklahoma has the highest rates of domestic violence and one of the worst maternal death rates nationwide. Not to mention, the state enforces one of the strictest abortion bans in the country, with no exceptions for rape or incest. And now, in the aftermath of Trump’s “Big Beautiful Bill,” **_most_ of Oklahoma's rural hospitals are at risk of closing.** As you can imagine, this has horrifying real-world consequences.

Despite all of that, the Republican frontrunner for Governor of Oklahoma – Charles McCall, the GOP Speaker of the Oklahoma House of Representatives – is doubling down on culture wars rather than responding to the needs of constituents in this deep red state. He recently released a disturbing commercial in which he slices a banana in half (???) while stating, “Gender is god-given, and there’s only two of ‘em.”

That’s not all: McCall’s campaign website brags about his record of passing the **_strongest anti-choice law in the nation_** , supporting legislation that attacks vaccines, holding an A rating from the NRA, banning sanctuary cities, erasing Critical Race Theory from schools, and much more.
---
[](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/121594647/589961338/-300884483?refcode=EM_F_251217worst_N_CD_90DO_13_G_N_P1&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9FQS9FQTAwNy8xLzg4NDk1IiwNCiAgIkRpc3RyaWJ1dGlvblVuaXF1ZUlkIjogIjQ4MGVkNjcxLWViZGMtZjAxMS04MTk0LTAwMGQzYTFlYzhjNiIsDQogICJFbWFpbEFkZHJlc3MiOiAiZHB3aWxsaXM2N0BnbWFpbC5jb20iDQp9&hmac=N6bMKYjSMlvgw4wwfs0BfxLhR522xlKt7URkuPNrs08=)
---
_Pictured: Charles McCall, surrounded by other male legislators, meeting about the state of Oklahoma's budget priorities for the year._
---
**But we have a plan:** if McCall wins the Republican primary, he will have a formidable and qualified Democratic WOMAN as his opponent. Her name is **Cyndi Munson** , and she is running a strong, well-organized campaign to be GOVERNOR of Oklahoma. Her Bold Move is honored to support her.

**Cyndi is nearing a make-or-break end-of-year fundraising goal for two reasons: 1.) The fundraising numbers her campaign posts by January will be essential to helping her secure more endorsements and early support. 2.) She will be unable to raise money for her campaign while she is in session in the OK state legislature beginning EARLY NEXT YEAR. Now is the time to contribute as we will be unable to do so starting in February!**

**With your help, we can block men like Charles McCall from passing even more harmful legislation – and bullying his own constituents.** [**Help improve the lives of so many women in Oklahoma by rushing a split donation of $20.26 or more TODAY!**](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/121594653/589961346/-300884483?refcode=EM_F_251217worst_N_CD_90DO_13_G_N_L1&amount=20.26&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9FQS9FQTAwNy8xLzg4NDk1IiwNCiAgIkRpc3RyaWJ1dGlvblVuaXF1ZUlkIjogIjQ4MGVkNjcxLWViZGMtZjAxMS04MTk0LTAwMGQzYTFlYzhjNiIsDQogICJFbWFpbEFkZHJlc3MiOiAiZHB3aWxsaXM2N0BnbWFpbC5jb20iDQp9&hmac=N6bMKYjSMlvgw4wwfs0BfxLhR522xlKt7URkuPNrs08=) _If you've saved your payment information with ActBlue Express, your donation will be processed immediately and automatically split between Her Bold Move and Cyndi Munson:_ | | [**$20.26 »**](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/121594660/589961353/-300884483?refcode=EM_F_251217worst_N_CD_90DO_13_G_N_EB20.26&express_lane=true&amount=20.26&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9FQS9FQTAwNy8xLzg4NDk1IiwNCiAgIkRpc3RyaWJ1dGlvblVuaXF1ZUlkIjogIjQ4MGVkNjcxLWViZGMtZjAxMS04MTk0LTAwMGQzYTFlYzhjNiIsDQogICJFbWFpbEFkZHJlc3MiOiAiZHB3aWxsaXM2N0BnbWFpbC5jb20iDQp9&hmac=N6bMKYjSMlvgw4wwfs0BfxLhR522xlKt7URkuPNrs08=)

**Unsurprisingly, Oklahoma is among the states with the lowest women’s representation in its state government. As outlined above, this underrepresentation is reflected in the state’s dangerous and draconian policies for women.**

As Governor, Cyndi will repeal Oklahoma’s abortion ban. As she’s been meeting with voters across her state, she’s found that voters on both sides of the aisle feel that the current anti-abortion laws do not meet their values and ultimately prevent too many women from getting the care they need.

Additionally, according to the 2024 March of Dimes report, 51.9% of Oklahomans do not have access to maternity healthcare, and an additional 18.2% have low/moderate access to healthcare. As Governor, Cyndi wants to increase access to maternity healthcare for women across the state and fully eliminate these healthcare deserts. Her plan is to introduce legislation that would increase funding for hospitals and assist in the recruitment of doctors who specialize in women’s healthcare.

**Here’s the thing:** Cyndi flipped a _majority Republican_ district and has kept it for the last 10 years – she’s actually increased her win margins election after election. She accomplished this by knocking on doors, having honest conversations, and staying true to her values. Now, she needs our help to keep up that momentum.

We’ve seen Democratic Governors win in “red” states – despite having Republican supermajorities in their State Houses and Senate chambers. Kentucky and Kansas, for example, have both elected popular Democratic Governors. **If anyone can do it, it’s Cyndi Munson.**

Peter, **You might have seen Becca on TV this week talking about what’s happening with Venezuela and the role Congress is supposed to play.** This comes as the U.S. has carried out military strikes in the Pacific and announced a blockade of sanctioned oil tankers involving Venezuela, escalating tensions in the region**without congressional approval.** **She was clear about one thing: no president gets to launch military action or put our Guard in harm’s way without authorization from Congress. That’s the law. And our Constitution doesn’t take the holidays off. Neither should accountability**. [**And if you believe in leaders who stand up for the Constitution, who hold power to its limits, and who fight for a government that follows the law, we hope you’ll consider chipping in right now. Even a small contribution — $5, $25, or whatever feels right — helps sustain this fight as we go into a crucial year.**](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/121595305/589963417/673394817?nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMTAvMS84MTM0MiIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI0YzBlZDY3MS1lYmRjLWYwMTEtODE5NC0wMDBkM2ExZWM4YzYiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=TVmid0CyRAOKG1HE1brWnU0zFk8MXoPaAEv3AMVrDwY=) Because here’s the bottom line: Becca’s voice doesn’t echo everywhere — but when she stands up on TV, in Congress, or in front of national audiences, she gives voice to Vermonters who expect leaders to be responsible, humane, and accountable. **We’re proud of her, and we think you should be too.** But doing this work —**standing up for the Guard families** , speaking out on unauthorized military actions, and defending constitutional process — takes funds. name: NEGOP
subject: Reminder: 🚨 Your Weekly Update From The NEGOP
disclaimer_text: Paid for by the Nebraska Republican Party 1610 N St
body:
News, events, and action items to keep Nebraska red and growing strong.
| | | | |
---
| Letter from the Chair
---
A Rising Tide Lifts All Boats
The timeless aphorism--"a rising tide lifts all boats"--reminds us that shared prosperity and strength benefit everyone. Just as a strong, prosperous America strengthens the world, a robust Republican Party strengthens our state and nation. In Nebraska, a unified and well-resourced Republican Party elevates all our candidates, volunteers, and conservative causes.
Individualism is a cornerstone of our philosophy--it's what drives innovation and personal responsibility. Yet, when we unite behind common principles, the result is far greater than the sum of our individual efforts. This is synergy: the multiplied power of collective action.
The Nebraska Republican Party has invested heavily in building a strong foundation for success. Through a comprehensive Political Plan and the dedicated leadership of our Political Director, Joe Hagerty, we have made unprecedented progress in recruiting principled, high-quality candidates. This year, we are ahead of schedule like never before, thanks to collaboration with political consultants, the Republican National Committee (RNC), current and former officeholders, county party chairmen, and countless grassroots supporters.
But recruiting excellent candidates is only the beginning. State parties play a unique and vital role in supporting elections, especially under federal and state campaign finance laws. Unlike PACs, which face strict limits on coordination:

* State parties can make coordinated expenditures directly with federal candidates in general elections, allowing seamless collaboration on strategy and resources.

* We can receive unlimited transfers from national party committees, pooling funds for maximum impact.

* Well-funded state parties enable synergistic activities that benefit the entire ticket: robust get-out-the-vote (GOTV) drives, voter registration initiatives, unified messaging, shared polling data, and much more.

Additionally, well-funded state parties can provide direct contributions to state-level candidates and county parties--supporting the grassroots infrastructure that PACs and individual donors cannot replicate as effectively. This positions the party as the central hub for funding, strategy, and victory.
As the saying goes, a rising tide lifts all boats. The corollary is equally true: a low tide exposes the rocks. When the party is underfunded or fragmented, weaknesses emerge, obstructions grow, and opportunities slip away.
Today, the Nebraska Republican Party stands at a pivotal moment--brimming with potential yet facing ongoing challenges. With your support, we can raise the tide: funding critical programs, empowering candidates up and down the ballot, and securing conservative victories for generations to come.
Will we seize this opportunity to lift all boats, or allow the rocks to be revealed? The choice is ours. Join us--donate, volunteer, and get involved today at [ne.gop.](https://feyrpzhbb.cc.rs6.net/tn.jsp?f=001VI9rBbTvSJftJXGUEA7WcNqa6JMjO6ybRVdQhP2Z3grp931j8W885duz4QcfYuhTZlpu8EjTNhc2bzlfvGWX3yjfJXYGdwbMamGMWZPx4XDU3Q5VJ7uMxeU9xNT_DnasZHITHplJSJs=&c=2MK90QGNkyja5zr7fF8Czs0__hWeB8xWh2ymuYwCX_atllF9ZBFUCA==&ch=o4TUBZkinLfUnha0jEObzJRTtfD2qE5CoRDDepcPMaojFqsfFu28dA==)
Together, we'll build a stronger Nebraska

Mary Jane Truemper Chairman, Nebraska Republican Party

clean_body: News, events, and action items to keep Nebraska red and growing strong. | | | | | | Letter from the Chair A Rising Tide Lifts All Boats The timeless aphorism--"a rising tide lifts all boats"--reminds us that shared prosperity and strength benefit everyone. Just as a strong, prosperous America strengthens the world, a robust Republican Party strengthens our state and nation. In Nebraska, a unified and well-resourced Republican Party elevates all our candidates, volunteers, and conservative causes. Individualism is a cornerstone of our philosophy--it's what drives innovation and personal responsibility. Yet, when we unite behind common principles, the result is far greater than the sum of our individual efforts. This is synergy: the multiplied power of collective action. The Nebraska Republican Party has invested heavily in building a strong foundation for success. Through a comprehensive Political Plan and the dedicated leadership of our Political Director, Joe Hagerty, we have made unprecedented progress in recruiting principled, high-quality candidates. This year, we are ahead of schedule like never before, thanks to collaboration with political consultants, the Republican National Committee (RNC), current and former officeholders, county party chairmen, and countless grassroots supporters. But recruiting excellent candidates is only the beginning. State parties play a unique and vital role in supporting elections, especially under federal and state campaign finance laws. Unlike PACs, which face strict limits on coordination: State parties can make coordinated expenditures directly with federal candidates in general elections, allowing seamless collaboration on strategy and resources. We can receive unlimited transfers from national party committees, pooling funds for maximum impact. * Well-funded state parties enable synergistic activities that benefit the entire ticket: robust get-out-the-vote (GOTV) drives, voter registration initiatives, unified messaging, shared polling data, and much more. Additionally, well-funded state parties can provide direct contributions to state-level candidates and county parties--supporting the grassroots infrastructure that PACs and individual donors cannot replicate as effectively. This positions the party as the central hub for funding, strategy, and victory. As the saying goes, a rising tide lifts all boats. The corollary is equally true: a low tide exposes the rocks. When the party is underfunded or fragmented, weaknesses emerge, obstructions grow, and opportunities slip away. Today, the Nebraska Republican Party stands at a pivotal moment--brimming with potential yet facing ongoing challenges. With your support, we can raise the tide: funding critical programs, empowering candidates up and down the ballot, and securing conservative victories for generations to come. Will we seize this opportunity to lift all boats, or allow the rocks to be revealed? The choice is ours. | | Dorian Gray glanced at the picture, and suddenly an uncontrollable feeling of hatred for Basil Hallward came over him, as though it had been suggested to him by the image on the canvas, whispered into his ear by those grinning lips. The mad passions of a hunted animal stirred within him, and he loathed the man who was seated at the table, more than in his whole life he had ever loathed anything. He glanced wildly around. Something glimmered on the top of the painted chest that faced him. His eye fell on it. He knew what it was. It was a knife that he had brought up, some days before, to cut a piece of cord, and had forgotten to take away with him. He moved slowly towards it, passing Hallward as he did so. As soon as he got behind him, he seized it and turned round. Hallward stirred in his chair as if he was going to rise. He rushed at him and dug the knife into the great vein that is behind the ear, crushing the man’s head down on the table and stabbing again and again. There was a stifled groan and the horrible sound of some one choking with blood. Three times the outstretched arms shot up convulsively, waving grotesque, stiff-fingered hands in the air. He stabbed him twice more, but the man did not move. Something began to trickle on the floor. He waited for a moment, still pressing the head down. Then he threw the knife on the table, and listened. He could hear nothing, but the drip, drip on the threadbare carpet. He opened the door and went out on the landing. The house was absolutely quiet. No one was about. For a few seconds he stood bending over the balustrade and peering down into the black seething well of darkness. Then he took out the key and returned to the room, locking himself in as he did so. The thing was still seated in the chair, straining over the table with bowed head, and humped back, and long fantastic arms. Had it not been for the red jagged tear in the neck and the clotted black pool that was slowly widening on the table, one would have said that the man was simply asleep. How quickly it had all been done! He felt strangely calm, and walking over to the window, opened it and stepped out on the balcony. The wind had blown the fog away, and the sky was like a monstrous peacock’s tail, starred with myriads of golden eyes. He looked down and saw the policeman going his rounds and flashing the long beam of his lantern on the doors of the silent houses. The crimson spot of a prowling hansom gleamed at the corner and then vanished. A woman in a fluttering shawl was creeping slowly by the railings, staggering as she went. Now and then she stopped and peered back. Once, she began to sing in a hoarse voice. The policeman strolled over and said something to her. She stumbled away, laughing. A bitter blast swept across the square. The gas-lamps flickered and became blue, and the leafless trees shook their black iron branches to and fro. He shivered and went back, closing the window behind him.
---

Putin didn’t see it coming.

In the middle of the day, a wave of Ukrainian AI drones launched from trucks and wiped out 41 Russian warplanes—caught on horrifying footage.
[ ](https://links.main.yourcapitalminds.com/u/click?_t=affa6bfca09a497f86454ba803e6a9a2&_m=6b8d31fde66f4b5b8713cb9843a52457&_e=1iSgW-TArWYlIwaiNv0fiNtXf5YkJd6ZzxZ-qGai7k4CwcLpJ5Fn501WClKVbUDWpXmwA6VhB1TxMdXp5YWF3W4KCWcOPxRZINfOJT3NFxY3p6Ck5NGbvdPSYghPwa_0r_sRQ-kOj1NBV6BhmHlYP3JwN_YoTAofPPBzWVy0_W3IGYOEU5r1kgCQAAd4etFIDtJmhZlxsQrurDVd5amf8ObN8BR9_w7GY_ZqItz9wMveFFTDuh3ihtXOUO5rwSVuO-MzoKwThtM4U7Do6wQiTv4Di2vGYNf1CukyTnziaRvOH8T8MCLYsGsARmxXwpBXIQtAqpKsR-zR5divfFGUGxTcxAx1Gc-Krz_sKelWHLiKeOL6WGTog7L8gq2XeuTPWZIKXydfHVsn8HWYHXv8hBK4dVY4KmxKX-cKmoW4kYf-PaLlYHvoD9pn8Ns9mu30oKZRP8fb4R-_IzO2VAm_uIOQSXyJ4nNQlEz9I961neiDnzuvLsxb3Nku2mqFJVk6_gtIQqB8IC8y9XWyQfdxFAAvLLgftSK3_8BjHpE7riGOIYb-AQZI9eQs6acT2uLkf2_xKCPaVai9t8z6VetllQ%3D%3D)
Tu-95s. Tu-22M3s. Gone in minutes. Over $7B in damage.

These weren’t just drones. This was black-ops AI tech that slipped past radar and hit with surgical precision.

Russia’s elite bombers? Vaporized.

The world is watching. The future of war is here.

[Find out what AI system made it possible >>>](https://links.main.yourcapitalminds.com/u/click?_t=affa6bfca09a497f86454ba803e6a9a2&_m=6b8d31fde66f4b5b8713cb9843a52457&_e=1iSgW-TArWYlIwaiNv0fiNtXf5YkJd6ZzxZ-qGai7k4CwcLpJ5Fn501WClKVbUDWpXmwA6VhB1TxMdXp5YWF3W4KCWcOPxRZINfOJT3NFxY3p6Ck5NGbvdPSYghPwa_0r_sRQ-kOj1NBV6BhmHlYP3JwN_YoTAofPPBzWVy0_W3IGYOEU5r1kgCQAAd4etFIwi_imMoTFtbzb3hZtVjhT48Nr2vvHOq9WbwhNWBwZMQ3vzA6gsP74hkOYg6chLcKobVCPhUu8-9iCeEgIzoHAaNs6qG3mi36AEHiu6MlJvRsk5Zd4i8ptBEkk_R8v-ZL4KVS_I4JcxlwtZvWG4zw7AAVS6jBGN_cKkhtbn5CWK-K2cRT7CqQqk4sw04AzwjiPS6n7Y7xUagyaiw3-qZwETrFBVV1QLk9yE3ErtRS2BdTuT4wknRXq1ccGhTKsm8wEuf1Bb4zjCGUSVwCI2J19WlW6C2LiVqcc-i3Oz4X8iH0pZqv4YG_jDBXjH36EYXuu_tnc6Id9FVMYytE9DAKDTRHFXwuwa7iN8XBieCQBdPv-WwT3i2q5hDYAoi8RKRq6pygjjOvmAZr2lTlMZh2yg%3D%3D)

**Simmy Adelman, Editor**
_Behind the Markets_
Ivan Dmytrovych Sirko was born in the early seventeenth century, approximately between 1605 and 1610. This is evidenced primarily by the results of research on Sirko's remains, according to which the Cossack ataman was 70-75 years old at the time of his death in 1680. The first researcher of Sirko's biography, the famous Ukrainian historian Dmytro Yavornytskyi, suggested that the future ataman was born in Sloboda Ukraine in the settlement of Merefa (now the city of Merefa, Kharkiv region). However, this is not true. Merefa was founded only in 1658, i.e. when Sirko was in his late teens. However, Sirko and his family lived in Merefa for a long time in his house, and after his death, his wife and some family members lived there. The fact that Merefa in Slobozhanshchyna is associated with the name of the Cossack Ataman is evidenced by another name for the town recorded in some Ukrainian chronicles, Sirkivka. Where, then, should we look for Sirko's homeland? In our opinion, the sources give grounds to localize it in Eastern Podillia, namely in the Vinnytsia region. It is worth recalling that Sirko was a colonel in Vinnytsia (Kalnytsia) in 1658-1660. As you know, at that time in Ukraine, people who came from the local area were usually elected to such positions. Let us also recall a letter from Hetman of the Left Bank Ukraine Ivan Samoilovych to the tsarist voivode Prince G. Romodanovsky of April 20, 1675*, which recounts (in Russian) Sirko's own words: "And since I was born among the Poles, I want to die there." Thus, it is clear that Sirko was born on the Ukrainian lands that were under the rule of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, not Muscovy (the latter included Slobozhanshchyna). The historian Volodymyr Borysenko's assumption that Sirko's birthplace was the hundred-man town of the Bratslav regiment Murafa, a few kilometers from Kalnyk, is correct. Now it is the village of Zhdanov, Vinnytsia region. By the way, Murafa in the Slobozhanshchyna probably originated from this Murafa, because immigrants from the Right Bank of Ukraine to the Left Bank often gave the newly founded settlements the names of their native villages or towns. It is also worth noting Sirko's great interest in Keleberda, which he founded in 1675 on the left bank of the Dnipro. For a long time, he sought from the Moscow tsar the right to own this town or sloboda, as well as the strategically important crossing of the Dnipro near Perevolochna (Sirko wanted this crossing not for himself, but for the entire Zaporozhian Lowland Army). Contrary to popular belief, we believe that Sirko did not come from a Cossack family, but from a small Ukrainian Orthodox gentry. For example, we know the name of a Podillia nobleman, Voytekh Sirko, who was married to a certain Olena Kozynska. Sources mention them in 1592. It is possible that they were relatives of the Cossack leader. Perhaps it is no coincidence that the kings of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, writing to Sirko, called him "urodzon." The word "nobleman" evokes negative associations for many people due to stereotypes instilled in school textbooks of the Stalinist era. However, this approach is not always correct. The term "nobleman" is identical to the words "knight," "chevalier," "nobleman," etc. and was commonly used not only in Poland but also in Ukraine, Belarus, and the Czech Republic. If scholars in Russia, Poland, and the Czech Republic, having contracted vulgar sociologism, no longer accuse Pushkin or Bunin, Suvorov or Kutuzov, Žižka or Kostiushko of being of noble or gentry origin, why have attacks on the Ukrainian and Belarusian gentry continued until recently? Is it not because in this way the imperial "divide and conquer" was transferred to our long-suffering history? Was it not because it was necessary to impose a nihilistic attitude toward the history of Ukraine, to "prove" that Ukrainians or Belarusians were just dark labor without national consciousness, ethnographic material doomed to build other people's empires? Meanwhile, the Ukrainian gentry produced many outstanding sons of Ukraine, fighters for its freedom. Suffice it to mention the names of Kryshtof Kosinski, Petro Konashevych-Sahaidachny, Ivan Sulyma, Ivan Mazepa, Bohdan Khmelnytsky, and his associates: Ivan and Danylo Vyhovsky, Ivan Bohun, Danylo and Ivan Nechai, Mykhailo Krychevsky, Jan Sokolovsky, Siluyan Muzhylovsky, and others. Sirko's appearance and personality do not always coincide with the conventional ideas about him that have developed in some works of art. The Cossack leader was slightly taller than average (174-176 cm), had regular facial features, and a straight nose. By the way, the great Relin intuitively caught this when he depicted him in the center of his painting "Cossacks Writing a Letter to the Turkish Sultan." It is interesting that the prototype for Sirko's image in the painting was the famous military leader General Dragomirov. Only one significant detail was not known to Repin and his consultant D. Yavornytskyi - a red birthmark on Sirko's lower lip on the right side. His contemporaries considered it a "sign of God" given to distinguish him from ordinary people. The Grand Hetman of the Crown, the future King of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, Jan Sobieski, who knew Sirko well and met him in Bratslav in 1671.

The wind had blown the fog away, and the sky was like a monstrous peacock’s tail, starred with myriads of golden eyes. He looked down and saw the policeman going his rounds and flashing the long beam of his lantern on the doors of the silent houses. The crimson spot of a prowling hansom gleamed at the corner and then vanished. A woman in a fluttering shawl was creeping slowly by the railings, staggering as she went. Now and then she stopped and peered back. Once, she began to sing in a hoarse voice. The policeman strolled over and said something to her. She stumbled away, laughing. A bitter blast swept across the square. The gas-lamps flickered and became blue, and the leafless trees shook their black iron branches to and fro. He shivered and went back, closing the window behind him. Dorian Gray glanced at the picture, and suddenly an uncontrollable feeling of hatred for Basil Hallward came over him, as though it had been suggested to him by the image on the canvas, whispered into his ear by those grinning lips. The mad passions of a hunted animal stirred within him, and he loathed the man who was seated at the table, more than in his whole life he had ever loathed anything. He glanced wildly around. Something glimmered on the top of the painted chest that faced him. His eye fell on it. He knew what it was. It was a knife that he had brought up, some days before, to cut a piece of cord, and had forgotten to take away with him. He moved slowly towards it, passing Hallward as he did so. As soon as he got behind him, he seized it and turned round. Hallward stirred in his chair as if he was going to rise. He rushed at him and dug the knife into the great vein that is behind the ear, crushing the man’s head down on the table and stabbing again and again. There was a stifled groan and the horrible sound of some one choking with blood. Three times the outstretched arms shot up convulsively, waving grotesque, stiff-fingered hands in the air. He stabbed him twice more, but the man did not move. Something began to trickle on the floor. He waited for a moment, still pressing the head down. Then he threw the knife on the table, and listened. He could hear nothing, but the drip, drip on the threadbare carpet. He opened the door and went out on the landing. The house was absolutely quiet. No one was about. For a few seconds he stood bending over the balustrade and peering down into the black seething well of darkness. Then he took out the key and returned to the room, locking himself in as he did so. The thing was still seated in the chair, straining over the table with bowed head, and humped back, and long fantastic arms. Had it not been for the red jagged tear in the neck and the clotted black pool that was slowly widening on the table, one would have said that the man was simply asleep. How quickly it had all been done! He felt strangely calm, and walking over to the window, opened it and stepped out on the balcony. The wind had blown the fog away, and the sky was like a monstrous peacock’s tail, starred with myriads of golden eyes. He looked down and saw the policeman going his rounds and flashing the long beam of his lantern on the doors of the silent houses. The crimson spot of a prowling hansom gleamed at the corner and then vanished. A woman in a fluttering shawl was creeping slowly by the railings, staggering as she went. Now and then she stopped and peered back. Once, she began to sing in a hoarse voice. The policeman strolled over and said something to her. She stumbled away, laughing. A bitter blast swept across the square. The gas-lamps flickered and became blue, and the leafless trees shook their black iron branches to and fro. He shivered and went back, closing the window behind him. | | | Putin didn’t see it coming. In the middle of the day, a wave of Ukrainian AI drones launched from trucks and wiped out 41 Russian warplanes—caught on horrifying footage. Tu-95s. Tu-22M3s. Gone in minutes. Over $7B in damage. These weren’t just drones. This was black-ops AI tech that slipped past radar and hit with surgical precision. Russia’s elite bombers? Vaporized. The world is watching. The future of war is here. Simmy Adelman, Editor Behind the Markets Ivan Dmytrovych Sirko was born in the early seventeenth century, approximately between 1605 and 1610. This is evidenced primarily by the results of research on Sirko's remains, according to which the Cossack ataman was 70-75 years old at the time of his death in 1680. The first researcher of Sirko's biography, the famous Ukrainian historian Dmytro Yavornytskyi, suggested that the future ataman was born in Sloboda Ukraine in the settlement of Merefa (now the city of Merefa, Kharkiv region). However, this is not true. Merefa was founded only in 1658, i.e. when Sirko was in his late teens. However, Sirko and his family lived in Merefa for a long time in his house, and after his death, his wife and some family members lived there. The fact that Merefa in Slobozhanshchyna is associated with the name of the Cossack Ataman is evidenced by another name for the town recorded in some Ukrainian chronicles, Sirkivka. Where, then, should we look for Sirko's homeland? In our opinion, the sources give grounds to localize it in Eastern Podillia, namely in the Vinnytsia region. It is worth recalling that Sirko was a colonel in Vinnytsia (Kalnytsia) in 1658-1660. As you know, at that time in Ukraine, people who came from the local area were usually elected to such positions. Let us also recall a letter from Hetman of the Left Bank Ukraine Ivan Samoilovych to the tsarist voivode Prince G. Romodanovsky of April 20, 1675*, which recounts (in Russian) Sirko's own words: "And since I was born among the Poles, I want to die there." Thus, it is clear that Sirko was born on the Ukrainian lands that were under the rule of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, not Muscovy (the latter included Slobozhanshchyna). The historian Volodymyr Borysenko's assumption that Sirko's birthplace was the hundred-man town of the Bratslav regiment Murafa, a few kilometers from Kalnyk, is correct. Now it is the village of Zhdanov, Vinnytsia region. By the way, Murafa in the Slobozhanshchyna probably originated from this Murafa, because immigrants from the Right Bank of Ukraine to the Left Bank often gave the newly founded settlements the names of their native villages or towns. It is also worth noting Sirko's great interest in Keleberda, which he founded in 1675 on the left bank of the Dnipro. For a long time, he sought from the Moscow tsar the right to own this town or sloboda, as well as the strategically important crossing of the Dnipro near Perevolochna (Sirko wanted this crossing not for himself, but for the entire Zaporozhian Lowland Army). Contrary to popular belief, we believe that Sirko did not come from a Cossack family, but from a small Ukrainian Orthodox gentry. For example, we know the name of a Podillia nobleman, Voytekh Sirko, who was married to a certain Olena Kozynska. Sources mention them in 1592. It is possible that they were relatives of the Cossack leader. Perhaps it is no coincidence that the kings of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, writing to Sirko, called him "urodzon." The word "nobleman" evokes negative associations for many people due to stereotypes instilled in school textbooks of the Stalinist era. However, this approach is not always correct. The term "nobleman" is identical to the words "knight," "chevalier," "nobleman," etc. and was commonly used not only in Poland but also in Ukraine, Belarus, and the Czech Republic. If scholars in Russia, Poland, and the Czech Republic, having contracted vulgar sociologism, no longer accuse Pushkin or Bunin, Suvorov or Kutuzov, Žižka or Kostiushko of being of noble or gentry origin, why have attacks on the Ukrainian and Belarusian gentry continued until recently? Is it not because in this way the imperial "divide and conquer" was transferred to our long-suffering history? Was it not because it was necessary to impose a nihilistic attitude toward the history of Ukraine, to "prove" that Ukrainians or Belarusians were just dark labor without national consciousness, ethnographic material doomed to build other people's empires? Meanwhile, the Ukrainian gentry produced many outstanding sons of Ukraine, fighters for its freedom. Suffice it to mention the names of Kryshtof Kosinski, Petro Konashevych-Sahaidachny, Ivan Sulyma, Ivan Mazepa, Bohdan Khmelnytsky, and his associates: Ivan and Danylo Vyhovsky, Ivan Bohun, Danylo and Ivan Nechai, Mykhailo Krychevsky, Jan Sokolovsky, Siluyan Muzhylovsky, and others. Sirko's appearance and personality do not always coincide with the conventional ideas about him that have developed in some works of art. The Cossack leader was slightly taller than average (174-176 cm), had regular facial features, and a straight nose. By the way, the great Relin intuitively caught this when he depicted him in the center of his painting "Cossacks Writing a Letter to the Turkish Sultan." It is interesting that the prototype for Sirko's image in the painting was the famous military leader General Dragomirov. Only one significant detail was not known to Repin and his consultant D. Yavornytskyi - a red birthmark on Sirko's lower lip on the right side. His contemporaries considered it a "sign of God" given to distinguish him from ordinary people. The Grand Hetman of the Crown, the future King of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, Jan Sobieski, who knew Sirko well and met him in Bratslav in 1671. | | You are receiving this newsletter from Your Capital Minds (YCM), an esteemed member of the Stark Media LLC family. clean_body: | | | | | | | We're fast approaching our end-of-year deadline, and we're still short of our goal. I challenged my grassroots team to raise $50,000 in December, and for two good reasons. | | | | First , we all have watched the democrat party rake in billionaire cash and devious dark money. With it, they wreak havoc up and down the ballot. But unlike them, I rely on small-dollar donations from grassroots patriots across America. Second , it shows momentum. A strong fundraising report shows that our message is resonating. It shows that people want protections from AI at work and at home, or that people recognize that protecting the family is paramount! Help fuel the fight and show strength by donating before the end of the year! Every single dollar helps! God bless, Josh Hawley | | | | | | | | | | | ---|---|---|---|--- | | | Paid for by Josh Hawley Victory Committee | | | | |
